sample_tweets.text: Most security teams have never tested whether their defences actually work.  Not in theory. Not against the threat actors that are actively targeting their industry.  Group-IB's Purple Teaming service changes that.  Unlike traditional penetration testing, Purple Teaming is a live, collaborative exercise. Our red team executes adversary scenarios while your defenders monitor, respond, and tune detection rules in real time. The gap between what your tools are supposed to do and what they actually do gets closed within a single engagement.  Every scenario is grounded in Group-IB's Threat Intelligence and mapped to MITRE ATT&CK® — drawn from 1,600+ high-tech cybercrime investigations conducted since 2003. That means your team isn't practising against a generic framework. They're training against the specific tactics, techniques, and procedures of threat actors targeting organisations like yours.  Exercises cover ransomware simulations, Active Directory attacks, supply chain compromise, and data exfiltration, delivered on-site, remotely, or hybrid, over one to eight weeks.  The result: measurably improved detection coverage, updated response playbooks, and a security team that has performed under pressure.  Purple Teaming is available now, globally, through Group-IB's Digital Crime Resistance Centers across Asia-Pacific, Europe, the Middle East and Africa, the Americas, and Central Asia.  🔗 Read More: link.group-ib.com/44mwafP

Hiberfil.sys is a forensic goldmine hiding in plain sight.  When Windows hibernates, it writes a compressed snapshot of physical RAM to C:\hiberfil.sys. This hidden system file preserves the entire memory state — running processes, network connections, decrypted data, and yes, even passwords that were in memory at that moment.  For incident responders, hiberfil.sys is a second chance. If you didn't capture a memory dump during live response, this file can fill that gap. It's especially valuable when investigating lateral movement, credential theft, or malware that was memory-resident.  Parsing tools include Volatility 3 (with the Windows hibernation layer) and Arsenal Hibernation Recon. Both can decompress and analyze the snapshot just like a standard memory image.  One critical detail: hiberfil.sys captures the system state at hibernation, not shutdown. If the machine was shut down normally, the file may be zeroed out or contain stale data. Always check timestamps and validate the content before analysis.  In MITRE ATT&CK terms, adversaries targeting T1005 (Data from Local System) may not realize they left a memory snapshot behind. That oversight can be your advantage.  #DFIR #DigitalForensics

Breaking into cyber threat intelligence remains one of the most frequently asked and least clearly answered questions among analysts starting their careers.  We have published a comprehensive guide setting out what the path into CTI actually looks like in 2026: the foundational skills, the certification progression, the tooling analysts are expected to know, and the applied practice that separates a certificate from demonstrable capability.  What the guide covers: - The core technical and analytical skills required before specializing in intelligence work - A structured certification path, from foundational credentials to advanced CTI-specific certifications - The frameworks and tooling used in day-to-day intelligence operations, including MITRE ATT&CK and OSINT tradecraft - Realistic salary expectations across experience levels - How to build demonstrable experience through applied practice rather than theory alone  Each stage is mapped to hands-on environments on our platform, including underground forum simulation, credential-leak analysis, and SOC investigation scenarios, so that learners can practice the workflow rather than only read about it. Threat Hunting and the Shift From Waiting to Searching  Threat hunting started with an uncomfortable realization: some of the most serious intrusions were not discovered because a product screamed. They were discovered because a skilled analyst went looking. In the years when investigators at firms like Mandiant were dissecting long-running intrusions and organizations like Target and OPM were learning how deeply attackers could burrow in, the lesson was becoming hard to ignore. A determined adversary could sit inside a network, use ordinary admin tools, move with stolen credentials, and stay quieter than many defenses were built to notice.  For more information, see the first comment below!  For a long time, security operations were organized around waiting. Wait for the antivirus hit. Wait for the SIEM correlation. Wait for the blocked IP, the malware signature, the obvious alarm. Threat hunting changed the question. Instead of asking what alert had fired, better teams started asking what should not be happening in this environment even if no tool had raised its hand yet.  That mattered because modern attackers rarely behave like movie villains smashing through the front door. They log in. They blend into PowerShell, RDP, remote management tools, identity systems, and cloud consoles. They look almost normal until someone notices the pattern: a new admin account at the wrong time, lateral movement that does not fit the user, DNS requests that make no business sense, authentication behavior that feels just slightly off. Hunting became the craft of finding that faint signal before it turned into a headline.  Frameworks like MITRE ATT&CK later helped formalize the work, but the real shift was cultural before it was procedural. Mature teams learned that detection is not just a technology problem. It is an operational mindset. If your security model depends on clear alarms arriving on time, what happens when the attacker knows exactly how to stay between them?  #Cybersecurity #ThreatHunting #BlueTeam #DetectionEngineering #IncidentResponse #SOC #MITREATTACK #ThreatDetection #SecurityOperations #InfoSec

4. “LNabu, and MITRE ATT&CK group G0122 — is an Iran-linked cyber-espionage cluster focused on universities, research institutions, government agencies, and private-sector targets. MITRE states the group has targeted research and proprietary data worldwide since at least 2013 and that Mabna Institute activity was conducted on behalf of Iran’s government, specifically the IRGC. (MITRE ATT&CK)  The U.S. Department of Justice indicted nine Iranians linked to Mabna Institute in 2018 for a mass cyber-theft campaign. Reporting and threat-intel summaries state the campaign compromised universities, companies, and government agencies, stole more than 31 terabytes of data, and caused an estimated US$3.4 billion in losses to universities. (Malpedia)  Proofpoint assessed that TA407/Silent Librarian continued targeting higher education after the indictments, using library-themed phishing lures, cloned university login portals, stolen branding, compromised accounts, and domains used for credential harvesting. (proofpoint.com)

T3MP3ST — framework for automated security analysis powered by an AI coding agent  T3MP3ST orchestrates reconnaissance, exploitation, and reporting stages via CLI or a War Room browser, turning the connected AI agent (Claude Code, Codex, Hermes) or local LLM into a guided vulnerability‑discovery tool.  Capabilities: • Multi‑agent architecture: 8 operators aligned with MITRE ATT&CK and Cyber Kill Chain phases. • Integration with local OpenAI‑compatible models without API keys (Ollama, LM Studio, vLLM). • Automatic result verification using "npm run verify-claims" and recalculation of all metrics based on execution artifacts. • Built‑in safeguards against out‑of‑scope actions. • API, MCP server, and an extensible arsenal of 35 tools (83 in the full set).  Compared to similar frameworks (e.g., "Metasploit Framework", "Cobalt Strike") "T3MP3ST" stands out with its cloud‑free, API‑key‑independent architecture and native LLM agent integration. However, the platform remains experimental: the effectiveness of its multi‑agent coordination has yet to be validated by comprehensive benchmarks, unlike traditional C2 frameworks.  Author: @elder_plinius Tool: github.com/elder-plinius/T3M… #dbugs_tools

sample_tweets.text: Major crypto exchange AscendEX has abruptly shut down operations as of July 1, leaving users unable to withdraw funds and the platform unresponsive to claims.  Once known as BitMax, the Singapore-headquartered platform rebranded in 2021 and positioned itself as a hub for both pro and retail traders.  After eight years in the game, it has now collapsed amid liquidity shortages in key assets like ethereum:native, solana:Es9vMFrzaCERmJfrF4H2FYD4KCoNkY11McCe8BenwNYB, and solana:So11111111111111111111111111111111111111112.  On-chain sleuth ZachXBT highlighted massive red flags, including depleted hot wallets across Ethereum, Solana, and Tron networks.  The exchange cited a mix of market pressures, financial struggles, operational challenges, and especially regulatory hurdles, pointing to its failure to secure MiCA authorization in the EU before the June 30 deadline.  The event comes years after the company survived a $77M Lazarus Group hack, showing how tough the current environment has become for centralized platforms.

ZachXBT Warning Followed by AscendEX Shutdown Announcement  Centralized crypto exchange AscendEX said it ceased operations on July 1, 2026, with accounts remaining available only for limited exit purposes. All withdrawal requests now require manual review and may be delayed, require additional information, or fail to be processed. ZachXBT had previously warned that AscendEX was facing multiple reports of delayed or unprocessed withdrawals, and said its public hot wallets appeared to lack major assets such as ETH, USDT and SOL.  Once a Top-10 CEX Backed by Polychain and Hack VC  Formerly known as BitMax, AscendEX was founded in 2018 by George Cao and Ariel Ling, and was once a top-10 centralized exchange by trading volume. Public information shows Cao holds a PhD in computer science from the University of Chicago. AscendEX raised a $50 million Series B round in 2021 led by Polychain Capital and Hack VC, and was also reported to have suffered a $78 million Lazarus Group-linked hack in December 2021.

If you wait for static tags to update, the funds are already gone.  Breaking the Gridlock with Dynamic Intel: Instead of waiting around for addresses to be officially labeled, security tracking firms utilized Dynamic Operational Intelligence to monitor newly created addresses executing high-frequency, specific-path cross-chain swaps in real time.  Real-World Attribution: Through Real-world Attribution, analysts pierced through the complex laundering network within milliseconds, precisely identifying that the ultimate destination of these funds was deposit addresses at several major CEX.

ethena – 2025 Year in Review February 2025 — First Major Resilience Test • February 21: @Bybit_Official suffers a hack of approximately $1.4B–$1.5B, attributed to the Lazarus Group. At the time, it was the largest hack in crypto history. • At that moment, @ethena had roughly $30M in unrealized PnL exposed on @Bybit_Official through its derivatives positions. This exposure was neutralized in under an hour. • The same day, $USDe experienced the largest redemption wave in its history: $120M–$123M withdrawn in just a few hours. On secondary markets, $USDe briefly dipped to $0.98 before quickly returning to peg. • Following the event, ethena immediately strengthened its risk management: •  Increased the mint/redeem buffer from $30M to $250M •  Reduced notional exposure to @Bybit_Official from 21% ($1.2B) to 15% ($800M) •  Redeployed a significant portion of positions to @binance • Shortly after, $ENA was listed on @BithumbOfficial . Why this event mattered This hack represented the first real-world, large-scale test of Ethena’s delta-neutral model. Despite the operational failure of one of its key trading partners, the protocol absorbed massive redemptions without service interruption or permanent peg loss, significantly strengthening its credibility. March 2025 — @convergeonchain Announcement • March 17-18: ethena and @Securitize unveil convergeonchain , an EVM-compatible blockchain dedicated to institutional assets, at the Tokenize Summit in New York. Target mainnet launch: Q2 2025. Initial announced partners include: •  @aave (Horizon) •  @pendle_fi •  @Morpho •  @maplefinance •ethereal (@meridiandotxyz) Key custodians announced: •  @Anchorage Digital •  @CopperHQ •  @FireblocksHQ •  @komainu •  @ZodiaCustody convergeonchain is designed to natively host: $USDe, $USDtb, and $iUSDe. April 2025 • April 17: Further technical details on convergeonchain . Architecture based on: •  @arbitrum •  @celestia for data availability Blocks produced every 100ms, with a target of 50ms by Q4. Testnet announced “in the coming weeks.” May 2025 • May 1 — TOKEN2049 Dubai Major partnership announced with The Open Network ($TON) and @telegram . $USDe and $sUSDe (renamed tsUSDe) become directly accessible in the @wallet_tg, potentially opening access to over a billion users. Early users receive a 10% APY bonus in $TON on the first 10,000 $tsUSDe per wallet. At this time, $USDe stands at approximately $4.6–4.7B, making it the 4th largest stablecoin globally. • Meanwhile, $ENA continues to decline even as protocol revenue grows from ~$10M to $20.5M weekly. This divergence between fundamentals and price appears driven mainly by investor concerns over future token unlocks rather than operational performance. June 2025 • June 2: coinbase adds $ENA to its listing roadmap. • Early June: $USDe reaches $5.9B–$6.3B in TVL and becomes the 3rd largest stablecoin, behind $USDT and $USDC. July 2025 • The GENIUS Act is passed in the United States, establishing a dedicated regulatory framework for stablecoins. • July 21: Announcement of @stablecoin_x , a dedicated $ENA treasury vehicle resulting from the merger of TLGY Acquisition Corp and SC Assets. Objectives: •  Nasdaq listing under ticker $USDE •  $360M raise Investors include: @PanteraCapital, @dragonfly_xyz , @GalaxyDigital , @polychaincap. Of the $360M raised: •  $260M to purchase $ENA on the open market •  @EthenaFndtn adds $60M worth of $ENA Following the announcement, $ENA reaches ~$0.58. September 2025 — The Pivotal Month • September 6: @stablecoin_x announces an additional $530M raise, bringing total announced funding to $890M. • September 9: @binance officially lists $USDe. New integrations include $USDE/ $USDT , $USDE/ $USDC pairs, @binance Earn, and collateral for perpetual contracts. $USDe becomes accessible to over 280 million users. Market cap approaches $12–13B. At this stage, the conditions for triggering the fee switch ( $USDe supply > $6B + presence on major exchanges) appear met. ethena communications indicate activation of the mechanism, though the exact operational rollout timeline remains under discussion. $ENA reaches ~$0.81, its highest level of the year. Other September announcements: •  Launch of $USDm with @megaeth •  Launch of $suiUSDe on @SuiNetwork •  $20M investment from M2 Capital October 2025 — The Major Stress Test • October 7: Partnership with UR Global. $USDe reaches its all-time high: over $14.8B. Three days later, one of the largest liquidation events in crypto history occurs. • October 10-11: Surprise announcement of new U.S. tariffs on China triggers: •  $Bitcoin crash from $125K to $102K (-18.4%) •  Nearly $19B in liquidations within 24 hours During the event: •  On Curve, $USDe remains almost stable (≈ -0.3%) •  On @binance , an extremely brief wick drops $USDe to $0.65 — largely attributed to a local order book or pricing glitch on the exchange, with no challenge to the protocol’s collateralization •  $ENA drops sharply but rebounds over 8% in the following days Why this event mattered Following the @Bybit_Official hack, this was the second major test of Ethena’s delta-neutral model. Despite massive liquidations, $USDe largely maintained its dollar peg market-wide. However, widespread deleveraging of $USDe strategies on @aave and @pendle_fi triggered a sharp contraction in TVL. November 2025 — The Contagion • November 3-4: @StreamDefi announces a $93M loss and suspends withdrawals. Its stablecoin $xUSD collapses. Contagion spreads to: @elixir , @Morpho , @eulerfinance , @compoundfinance , @SiloFinance . Total estimated exposure: ~$285M. This becomes the largest capital outflow from yield-bearing stablecoins since the $TerraUSD collapse in 2022. Key point: $USDe does not lose its dollar peg during the crisis. The protocol still sees ~$700M in redemptions over one week. Since the October 11 peak, over $5.4B has gradually left $USDe. @gdog97_ publicly highlights that Stream relied on a single opaque fund manager, implicitly underscoring the on-chain transparency of Ethena’s model. December 2025 • December 2: @Terminal_fi, the DEX incubated by ethena for convergeonchain , cancels its launch. All $280M deposited are fully refunded. At this point, no mainnet launch date for convergeeonchain has been announced, and public communications from the project have become much less frequent since the end of summer. • December 4: Partnership with @Anchorage Digital for institutional rewards on $USDtb and $USDe. • December 8: ethena repurchases 25 million $ENA (≈$7.05M), bringing its treasury to 779.89 million $ENA. • December 24: @Anchorage Digital Bank becomes the official custodian for $USDe reserves starting in 2026. • End of December: TVL falls to approximately $7.4B — nearly half of the October peak. 2025 Year in Numbers •  Protocol revenue: $230.8M •  $USDe: ~$6B → $14.8B → $7.4B •  $ENA: ~$1.25 at the start of the year → $0.81 in September → $0.20–$0.23 by end of December •  convergeonchain : Announced in March with Q2 target, still not live by year-end Conclusion 2025 will be remembered as a year of contrasts for ethena . Operationally, the protocol demonstrated remarkable resilience. It survived the largest hack in crypto history, absorbed multiple massive redemption waves, withstood one of the biggest liquidation events in the market, and overall maintained $USDe’s dollar peg. Adoption accelerated thanks to major exchange integrations, key institutional partnerships, and strong revenue growth. However, this operational strength did not translate into a sustained re-rating of $ENA. Token unlock concerns, delays with convergeonchain , uncertainty around the fee switch timeline, and large capital outflows after the October shock kept persistent pressure on the token price. Ultimately, 2025 was the year Ethena’s economic model was broadly validated under extreme market conditions, while $ENA’s valuation remained primarily tied to the team’s ability to execute its roadmap.

BAGO: Nagsagawa ang Hilagang Korea ng ilang pagsubok sa armas noong Hunyo 25 gamit ang iba't ibang pakete ng pag-atake, na posibleng para guluhin ang kakayahan ng US at Timog Korea sa pagtukoy ng paglulunsad, na makikinabang sa Hilagang Korea sa isang senaryo ng tunggalian. Nahihirapan pa rin ang Hilagang Korea na gayahin ang mga pakete ng pag-atake ng Russia, bagaman maaari itong makakuha ng karagdagang benepisyo mula sa karanasan at teknikal na kadalubhasaan ng Russia sa hinaharap.  Iba pang Mahahalagang Tala: Ang PRC, Hilagang Korea, at Russia ay nagsagawa ng paglabag sa himpapawid at dagat sa ADIZ ng Timog Korea at malapit sa NLL noong Hunyo 27, na nag-udyok ng tugon mula sa Timog Korea. Ipinapahiwatig ng PRC at Russia ang kanilang pagnanais para sa mas malaking kooperasyon sa Hilagang Korea at ang kanilang kahandaang salubungin ang teritoryo ng Timog Korea.  isw.pub/KoreanPeninsula07072…  Maaaring nagsusubok ang Hilagang Korea ng mga strike package na idinisenyo upang guluhin ang pagtuklas ng paglulunsad ng US at Timog Korea.   Maaaring gamitin ng Hilagang Korea ang mas sari-saring kakayahan sa pag-atake upang mabawasan ang bisa ng mga depensa ng misil ng US at Timog Korea sa panahon ng tunggalian.  Nagsagawa ang Hilagang Korea ng ilang pagsubok sa armas noong Hunyo 25, kabilang ang paglulunsad ng isang taktikal na ballistic missile, isang pinahusay na 240mm multiple launch rocket system, at isang projectile na may pinalawig na saklaw para sa isang 155mm self-propelled howitzer.[1] Iniulat ng media ng Timog Korea noong Hunyo 29 na ang Joint Chiefs of Staff ng Timog Korea ay hindi karaniwang nahuli sa pag-uulat ng paglulunsad ng ballistic missile sa Press Corps ng Kagawaran ng Pambansang Tanggulan. [2] Higit pang hinulaan ng media sa Timog Korea na ang pagsisikap ng Hilagang Korea na bumuo ng iba't ibang paraan at kakayahan sa paglulunsad ay nag-ambag sa kahirapan ng Timog Korea na matukoy at masuri ang paglulunsad.[3] Tinaya ng ISW-CDOT na layunin ng Hilagang Korea na pag-iba-ibahin ang kakayahan nitong maglunsad ng mga misil upang makalikha ng mga pakete ng pag-atake na mas mahirap para sa Timog Korea na pigilan. [4] Maaaring natututo ang Hilagang Korea mula sa mga pag-atake ng misil ng Russia sa Ukraine na pinagsasama ang mga ballistic, quasi-ballistic, at cruise missile kasama ang mga long-range drone upang lubos na mapahirapan ang malawakang pag-atake sa depensang panghimpapawid ng Ukraine. [5] Ang pagsisikap ng Hilagang Korea na bumuo ng ilang uri ng ballistic missile, kabilang ang mga may kakayahan sa pagliko habang nasa himpapawid, pati na rin ng mga cruise missile na inilulunsad mula sa lupa at dagat, ay maaaring magbigay-daan sa kanila na makabuo ng mas kumplikadong pakete ng pag-atake na magpapalaki ng pasanin sa depensang panghimpapawid ng Timog Korea. [6] Hindi pa nagpapakita ang Hilagang Korea ng kakayahan sa paggamit ng malayong-saklaw na strike drone, bagaman inangkin ng dating pinuno ng Intelihensiya ng Ukraine, si Kyrylo Budanov, noong Hunyo 2025 na tinutulungan ng Russia ang mga pagsisikap ng Hilagang Korea na gumawa ng katulad na mga drone.[7] Nanatili ang malalaking hamon sa mga pagsisikap ng Hilagang Korea na gayahin ang mga Russian strike package sa Ukraine. Mahina ang pagganap ng mga misil ng Hilagang Korea sa Ukraine bago pinabuti ng mga teknikal na pag-upgrade ng Russia ang kanilang katumpakan at nabawasan ang mga pagkasira habang nasa himpapawid. [8] Malamang na kulang ang Hilagang Korea sa masalimuot na imprastruktura para sa pagtutok upang suportahan ang mga kakayahan sa tumpak na pag-atake. [9] Gayunpaman, ang suporta ng Hilagang Korea sa digmaan ng Russia sa Ukraine ay maaaring magbigay-daan para makinabang ito mula sa karanasan at teknikal na kadalubhasaan ng Russia upang suportahan ang ambisyon nitong bumuo ng mas kumplikadong kakayahan sa tumpak na pag-atake.[10] Ang ganitong kakayahan ay maaaring pahintulutan ang Hilagang Korea na atakihin ang mahahalagang imprastrukturang militar sa buong Peninsula ng Korea sa panahon ng isang tunggalian o pahintulutan ang Hilagang Korea na magbanta sa mga lugar na siksik ang populasyon sa Timog Korea para sa layunin ng pagpigil o pamimilit. [11] [Ang sumusunod na teksto ay lumabas sa China & Taiwan Update, Hulyo 2, 2026] Ang paglusob ng himpapawid at pandagat mula sa People's Republic of China (PRC), Russia, at Hilagang Korea sa Air Defense Identification Zone (ADIZ) at mga tubig malapit sa Northern Limit Line (NLL) ng Timog Korea ay nag-udyok ng mga tugon militar at diplomatiko.Inakusahan ng Timog Korea na humigit-kumulang 10 sasakyang panghimpapawid militar ng PRC at Russia ang pumasok sa ADIZ nito, ngunit hindi sa himpapawid ng Timog Korea, para sa isang pinagsamang pangmatagalang patrulya noong Hunyo 27, na nag-udyok sa Timog Korea na magpadala ng mga eroplano ng fighter jet.[12] Ang pangmatagalang patrulyang ito ang ika-11 sa ganitong uri sa pagitan ng puwersang panghimpapawid ng PRC at Russia kasunod ng isang patrulya noong Disyembre 2025.[13]  Tumugon din ang Timog Korea sa mga bangkang pangisda ng PRC at Hilagang Korea na lumalapit at nagsasagawa ng ilegal na gawain malapit sa NLL nitong mga nakaraang linggo, kung saan iniulat ng Coast Guard ng Timog Korea ang kahirapan sa pagtugon sa mga bangka nang hindi pinupukaw ang Hilagang Korea.  [14] Ang mga pinagsamang operasyon sa himpapawid na ito ay nagpapahiwatig ng mas malaking kahandaan ng PRC at Russia na lusubin ang teritoryo ng Timog Korea, kung saan ang koordinasyon ng puwersang pandagat ng PRC at Hilagang Korea ay pinakamalinaw na nagpapahiwatig ng mas malaking suporta para sa Hilagang Korea, pati na rin ang pagpapalago ng interes ng PRC.  Mahahalagang Tala Pagyayaman ng Militar ng Hilagang Korea: Nagsagawa ang Hilagang Korea ng ilang pagsubok sa armas noong Hunyo 25 gamit ang iba't ibang pakete ng pag-atake, na posibleng para guluhin ang kakayahan ng US at Timog Korea sa pagtukoy ng paglulunsad, na makikinabang sa Hilagang Korea sa isang senaryo ng tunggalian. Nahihirapan pa rin ang Hilagang Korea na gayahin ang mga pakete ng pag-atake ng Russia, bagaman maaari itong makakuha ng karagdagang benepisyo mula sa karanasan at teknikal na kadalubhasaan ng Russia sa hinaharap.Pinagsamang Ehersisyo ng PRC-Russia-North Korea: Isinagawa ng PRC, North Korea, at Russia ang mga paglabag sa himpapawid at pandagat sa ADIZ ng South Korea at malapit sa NLL noong Hunyo 27, na nag-udyok ng tugon mula sa South Korea.   Ipinapahiwatig ng PRC at Russia ang kanilang hangarin para sa mas malaking kooperasyon sa North Korea at ang kanilang kahandaang lumusob sa teritoryo ng South Korea. Panloob na Pulitika ng North Korea Walang makabuluhang iniulat.  Mga Pag-unlad sa Militar ng Hilagang Korea Layunin ng Hilagang Korea na ilunsad ang pangalawa nitong destroyer sa loob ng dalawang buwan, na magpapahintulot dito na mag-deploy ng mga green-water assets sa parehong baybayin nito. Maaaring mahirapan ang Hilagang Korea na balansehin ang pagpapalawak ng umuusbong nitong green-water surface fleet at ang pagbuo ng mga pasilidad na suporta na kinakailangan upang gawing lubos na epektibo ang mga naturang barkong pandigma. Nagsagawa ang Hilagang Korea ng ilang pagsubok sa armas para sa guided missile destroyer na Kang Kon noong Hulyo 3 na kinabibilangan ng pagsusuri sa kakayahan nitong maglunsad ng "strategic" cruise missile.  [15] Inilunsad ang Kang Kon noong Mayo 2025 ngunit nabaliktad ito sa panahon ng seremonya ng paglulunsad at halos hindi naging aktibo hanggang sa isang pagsubok sa pag-navigate noong Hunyo 2026.[16] Ang kapatid na barko ng Kang Kon, ang Choe Hyon, ay opisyal na isinama sa West Sea Fleet ng Korean People's Army Navy (KPAN) noong Hunyo matapos ang ilang buwang pagsubok sa armas at pag-navigate. [17] Nanawagan si Heneral na Kalihim ng Partido ng mga Manggagawa ng Korea (WPK) Kim Jong Un na makumpleto ang pagsubok sa Kang Kon sa takdang oras para sa komisyoning nito sa Setyembre. [18] Naunang tinaya ng ISW-CDOT na sisikaping pabilisin ni Kim ang iskedyul ng komisyon ng Kang Kon upang higit pang isulong ang kanyang layunin na bumuo ng isang hukbong-dagat na berde-tubig.[19] Ang hukbong-dagat na berde-tubig ay responsable sa depensa sa baybayin at tabing-dagat habang may kakayahan din para sa limitadong pag-deploy sa labas ng mga baybaying-dagat ng isang bansa. [20] Malamang na ipapasok ng Hilagang Korea ang Kang Kon sa East Sea Fleet nito upang matiyak na may kakayahan itong green-water sa parehong baybayin nito.  Gayunpaman, kasalukuyang kulang ang Hilagang Korea sa makabuluhang pasilidad para sa pagpapanatili at suporta ng hukbong-dagat sa alinmang baybayin nito.[21] Ang kakulangang ito ang nagtulak kay Kim na manawagan ng pagtatayo ng mga bagong himpilan ng hukbong-dagat at mga shipyard habang isinasagawa ang pagsubok sa Kang Kon. [22] Ang KPAN ay mananatiling isang puwersang 'brown-water', o isang hukbong-dagat na pangunahing responsable sa pagtatanggol sa baybayin, hanggang sa mabuo nito ang imprastruktura para sa pagbabase at pagpapanatili na kailangan upang suportahan ang mga deployment sa labas ng kanilang teritoryo ng malalaking barkong pandigma sa ibabaw ng tubig.[23] Maaaring kailanganin ng Hilagang Korea na limitahan ang kanilang ambisyosong programa sa pagtatayo ng barko hanggang sa makabuo sila ng mga pasilidad para sa suporta ng hukbong-dagat at mas maliliit na sasakyang-dagat upang mapanatili ang mga pangmatagalang deployment.Hinikayat ni Kim ang industriya ng paggawa ng barko ng Hilagang Korea na maghahatid ng kabuuang 12 destroyer ng klase ng Choe Hyon pagsapit ng katapusan ng 2030 sa Ikasiyam na Kongreso ng Partido ng WPK noong Pebrero.[24] Mula noon, inilipat ni Kim ang kanyang pansin sa pagbuo ng isang 10,000-toneladang guided missile cruiser, na doble ang laki ng klase ng Choe Hyon, na malamang ay para mapalakas ang nuklear na pagpigil ng KPAN. [25] Ang pag-aalala ni Kim tungkol sa kakulangan ng mga pasilidad at kakayahan sa pagpapanatili para sa dalawang destroyer lamang ng klase ng Choe Hyon ay nagpapahiwatig na malamang na kakailanganin ng Hilagang Korea na sumailalim sa isang makabuluhang modernisasyon at pagsisikap sa konstruksyon upang suportahan ang mga barkong nais buuin at ilunsad ni Kim.  Maaari nitong pilitin ang Hilagang Korea na limitahan ang mga ambisyon nito para sa kagamitang pandagat pabor sa mga suportang kagamitan o makabuo ng isang hukbong pandagat na nagsisilbing simbolo ng kapangyarihan ng Hilagang Korea kaysa isang tunay na puwersang pandigma.  Posibleng ilegal na nakakakuha ang Hilagang Korea ng mga advanced na graphics card upang palawakin ang kanilang mga mapang-atake na operasyon ng cybercrime laban sa mga manggagawa sa Information Technology (IT) upang pondohan ang pagpapaunlad ng militar. Patuloy na mahigpit na nakikipagtulungan ang Estados Unidos, Timog Korea, at Japan upang hadlangan ang mga cyber na operasyon ng Hilagang Korea.Pinag-usapan ng Estados Unidos, Timog Korea, at Japan ang mga hakbang upang pigilan ang mga krimeng cyber ng Hilagang Korea, na nagpopondo sa mga sandatang pangmasasakla (WMD) at programa ng ballistic missile ng Hilagang Korea, sa isang pagpupulong ng diplomatic working group noong Hunyo 27.[26] Binigyang-diin ng tatlong bansa ang malubhang panganib na dulot ng pagnanakaw ng cryptocurrency ng Hilagang Korea at binigyang-diin ang pangangailangan para sa kooperasyon at koordinasyon ng tatlong bansa upang palakasin ang mga pandaigdigang sanksyon laban sa Hilagang Korea. [27] Iniulat ng The Wall Street Journal noong Hulyo 4 na gumagamit ang Hilagang Korea ng cryptocurrency upang bumili ng panggatong at kagamitan militar.[28] Nakalikha ang Hilagang Korea ng humigit-kumulang $2 bilyon mula sa pagnanakaw ng cryptocurrency noong 2025, halos doble ng halaga nito noong 2024, ayon sa Multilateral Sanctions Monitoring Team (MSMT).[29]Patuloy na minomonitor ng Tanggapan para sa Kontrol ng Dayuhang Ari-arian (Office of Foreign Assets Control) ng Kagawaran ng Pananalapi ng Estados Unidos ang pagpopondo ng Hilagang Korea sa mga programang WMD sa pamamagitan ng cryptocurrency at nagpataw ng mga parusa sa mga mamamayan ng Hilagang Korea at iba pang kasabwat na dayuhang entidad.[30] Naglabas ang Microsoft ng isang ulat sa seguridad noong Hulyo 17 na nagsasaad na ang grupong hacker ng Hilagang Korea na "Sapphire Sleet" ay naglunsad ng mga pag-atake sa supply-chain laban sa hindi bababa sa 140 na software package para sa mga kumpanyang may kaugnayan sa venture capital at blockchain. [31] Kinumpirma rin ng kumpanyang pang-cybersecurity na FortiGuard Labs na namamahagi ang mga hacker ng Hilagang Korea ng malisyosong software sa pamamagitan ng open-source na platapormang GitHub upang tutukan ang mas malawak na madla, kabilang ang mga indibidwal na gumagamit ng plataporma.[32] Iniulat ng Institute for National Security and Strategy (INSS), isang state-run na think tank ng Timog Korea, na nakakakuha ang Hilagang Korea ng mga graphics card na kontrolado sa pag-export sa pamamagitan ng mga iligal na network sa mga bansa tulad ng PRC. [33] Ibinunyag ng INSS na kasalukuyang ginagamit ng Hilagang Korea ang NVIDIA Tesla P100, GeForce RTX 2070, at Qualcomm Snapdragon 820 para sa pagpapaunlad ng artipisyal na intelihensiya (AI) at mga aplikasyong militar.[34] Naunang tinaya ng ISW-CDOT na lalong ginagamit ng Hilagang Korea ang AI upang magnakaw ng cryptocurrency at intelihensiya mula sa mga Kanlurang kumpanyang IT. [35] Ang mga IT worker na kaakibat ng Hilagang Korea ay ginagamit ang mga teknolohiyang AI upang gumawa ng mga phishing email at magpeke ng mga pekeng pagkakakilanlan upang lokohin ang mga kumpanya, pagkatapos ay hinihiling ang bayad na sahod sa cryptocurrency o isinasagawa ang mga operasyon upang kunin ang datos ng korporasyon.[36] Sinabi ng INSS na malamang na sasamantalahin ng Hilagang Korea ang mga teknolohiyang ito upang pasimplehin ang malawakang pag-uuri ng datos, i-automate ang paggawa ng mga phishing na mensahe, at padaliin ang paglikha at pamamahagi ng malisyosong software.[37]  Tila ginagamit ng Hilagang Korea ang mga advanced na teknolohiya upang pagbutihin ang mga cyberattack nito. Pangunahing tinarget ng Hilagang Korea ang mga cryptocurrency exchange at mga online na mangangalakal para sa malalaking, isang beses na pagnanakaw.[38] Ang grupong hacking na kaakibat ng Hilagang Korea na Lazarus ay nagnakaw ng humigit-kumulang $37 milyon mula sa pinakamalaking cryptocurrency exchange ng Timog Korea, ang UpBit, at nasangkot sa isang insidente ng cyber theft na nagkakahalaga ng $1.5 bilyon na tinarget ang pandaigdigang exchange na ByBit, noong 2025. [39] Madalas kumuha ang mga IT developer at kumpanya ng code at software mula sa mga open-source na website, na nagbibigay ng pagkakataon para samantalahin ng Hilagang Korea ang kanilang pag-access sa blockchain.[40] Pinapalawak ng mga threat actor ng Hilagang Korea ang kanilang mga oportunistikong pag-atake lampas sa mga partikular na estratehikong target sa pamamagitan ng walang piniling pamamahagi ng malisyosong software sa iba't ibang platform. Ang mga gawi sa pag-target ng Hilagang Korea ay nagpapahiwatig na inuuna nito ang banta ng cryptocurrency kaysa sa tradisyonal na cyberespionage o mga operasyong pang-intelihensiya, marahil upang unahin ang pagbuo ng kita at maiwasan ang pagtuklas sa pamamagitan ng mga pamamaraan ng beripikasyon ng pagkakakilanlan ng pamahalaan.  [41] Ugnayang Panlabas ng Hilagang Korea Maaaring pinapabilis ng Hilagang Korea ang pagpapanumbalik ng matibay nitong bilateral na ugnayan sa PRC. Nagpadala si Heneral na Kalihim ng WPK na si Kim Jong Un ng isang mensahe ng pagbati kay Heneral na Kalihim ng Partido Komunista ng Tsina (CCP) na si Xi Jinping noong Hulyo 1 upang gunitain ang ika-105 na anibersaryo ng CCP.[42] Ipinahayag ni Kim ang kanyang hangaring palakasin ang bilateral na ugnayan ng Hilagang Korea at PRC. [43] Sumagot si Xi kay Kim sa parehong araw na iyon, na maaaring sumasalamin sa hangarin ni Xi na panatilihin ang positibong takbo sa ugnayan ng PRC at Hilagang Korea.[44] Pinamunuan ni Tagapangulo ng Standing Committee ng Supreme People's Assembly na si Jo Yong Won, na itinuturing na kanang-kamay ni Kim Jong Un, ang isang delegasyon ng mga mataas na opisyal ng Hilagang Korea sa Embahada ng PRC sa Pyongyang upang dumalo sa okasyong paggunita. [45] Ang paggunitang ito ay kasunod ng pagbisita ni Xi sa Pyongyang noong Hunyo, ang kanyang unang paglalakbay sa Hilagang Korea mula pa noong 2019.[46] Nagkasundo sina Kim at Xi na palawakin ang palitan sa pulitika, diplomasya, militar, at sibilyan, ngunit hindi tinalakay ang denuklearisasyon at ang programang nukleyar ng Hilagang Korea, na sumasalamin sa tahimik na pagtanggap ni Xi sa nukleyar na katayuan ng Hilagang Korea. [47] Ang tahimik na pagtanggap ng PRC ay malamang na nag-ambag sa pagnanais ng Hilagang Korea na pagandahin ang ugnayan nito sa PRC, dahil sa sentral na papel ng mga sandatang nukleyar sa estratehiya sa pagtatanggol ng Hilagang Korea. Pinapalawak din ng dalawang bansa ang palitan sa sibilyan at kalakalan. Ang kalakalan ng PRC at Hilagang Korea noong Abril ay umabot sa pinakamataas na buwanang bilang mula pa noong Nobyembre 2017.Muling sinimulan ng dalawang bansa ang mga flight para sa pasahero at serbisyo sa riles noong Marso, na ipinahinto noong panahon ng pandemya ng COVID-19.[48] Ang malawakang pag-uulat ng state media ng Hilagang Korea sa mga mensahe nina Kim at Xi ay nagpapahiwatig na binabago ng Hilagang Korea ang paninindigan nito patungong Beijing tungo sa diplomatikong paglapit.  Patuloy na binigyang-diin ng media ng Hilagang Korea ang pagkaka-alyado ni Kim sa Russia at kay Pangulong Vladimir Putin ng Russia hanggang sa unang bahagi ng taon, habang binabawasan ang pag-uulat tungkol sa PRC at kay Xi sa mga maikling ulat lamang. [49] Maaaring ito ay isang pagsisikap ng Hilagang Korea na magbigay ng pantay na pagbabalita sa parehong estado, na sumasagisag sa balanseng pananaw ng Hilagang Korea sa dalawa nitong kaalyado. Patuloy na pinapaunlad ng Hilagang Korea ang ugnayan nito sa parehong Moscow at Beijing habang nag-aatubili pa ring makipagdayalogo sa Estados Unidos o Timog Korea.Malamang na susulitin ng Hilagang Korea ang estratehikong heopolitikal nitong kahalagahan at paglikha ng kita upang matiyak ang pag-iral ng rehimen sa pamamagitan ng pagpapanatili ng matibay na ugnayan sa PRC at Russia. Sinisamantala ng Hilagang Korea ang mahinang pagpapatupad ng mga parusa at ang kooperasyon sa Russia at PRC upang palawakin ang pag-export nito ng karbon sa ibang bansa bilang pangunahing pinagkukunan ng kita para pondohan ang pag-unlad at pagkuha ng mga sandata nito. Iniulat ng Citizens' Alliance for North Korean Human Rights (NKHR), isang non-governmental organization na nakabase sa Seoul, noong Hunyo 30 na ang bilang ng mga barkong Hilagang Koreano na nagdadala ng mga mineral, partikular na karbon, patungong Russia at PRC ay tumaas mula nang muling pagbubukas ng kanilang mga hangganan noong 2023 kasunod ng pandemya ng COVID-19.  [50] Iniulat ng NKHR na nakakilala ito ng 3,756 na sasakyang pandagat ng Hilagang Korea sa mga pantalan sa buong Hilagang Korea noong 2025, tumaas mula sa 1,929 noong 2023 at 3,079 noong 2024. [51] Ang mabilis na pagtaas sa pagitan ng 2023 at 2024 ay kasunod ng pag-veto ng Russia noong 2024 na nagwakas sa Panel of Experts ng United Nations (UN) na responsable sa pagsubaybay sa mga aktibidad ng Hilagang Korea sa pag-iwas sa mga parusa.[52]Illegal ang pag-export ng karbon ng Hilagang Korea sa ilalim ng Resolusyon 2371 ng UN Security Council. Nakilala ng ulat ang mga paglilipat mula barko-papuntang-barko, maling paglalagay ng bandila sa barko, at ang pag-iimbento sa pinagmulan ng karbon ng Hilagang Korea bilang mga madalas na taktika na ginagamit ng Hilagang Korea.[53] Binigyang-diin din ng ulat na ang Kagawaran ng Pambansang Tanggulan (MND) ng Hilagang Korea ang namomonopolyo sa pag-export ng karbon, na tinitiyak ang isang independiyenteng pinagkukunan ng kita sa labas ng pambansang badyet na nagpopondo sa pag-unlad at pagbili ng mga sandata.  Itinanghal ng WPK ang produksyon ng karbon bilang isang estratehikong prayoridad sa Ikalawang Pinalawak na Pulong ng Ikasiyam na Komite Sentral noong Hunyo 2026. Lalo pang tinatalakay ng mga pahayagang pampamahalaan ng Hilagang Korea ang industriya ng karbon, pinupuri ang "boluntaryong" pagmumulto ng mga kabataang Hilagang Koreano upang magtrabaho sa mga minahan ng karbon. Malamang na sasamantalahin ng Hilagang Korea ang paghina ng pagpapatupad ng mga pandaigdigang parusa at ang lumalalim na ugnayan sa Russia at PRC upang palawakin ang pag-export ng karbon at makakuha ng dayuhang salapi.[54] Ang pinatibay na kakayahan ng MND na kumita ay malamang na popondohan ang pag-unlad nito ng mga sandatang pangmasasakla. Naunang tinaya ng ISW-CDOT na maaaring nakararanas ang Hilagang Korea ng kakulangan sa produksyon ng karbon kumpara sa pangangailangan ng limang-taong plano sa pagpapaunlad at ng pagmamanupaktura ng depensa.[55] Ang alokasyon ng karbon na nakatuon sa pag-export ay maaaring magpalala ng kakulangan.  Hinihikayat ng Russia ang turismo sa Hilagang Korea sa pamamagitan ng pagtatatag ng bagong rutang bus na tumatawid sa hangganan. Sinabi ni Yevgeny Volosastov, pansamantalang kinatawan ng Kagawaran ng Ugnayang Panlabas ng Russia, na sisimulan ng Russian travel agency na Vostok Intur ang regular na serbisyo ng rutang bus mula Russia papuntang Hilagang Korea sa katapusan ng 2026 sa Khasan-Tumangang Bridge, isang bagong tulay para sa sasakyan sa Ilog Tumen.  [56] Iniulat ng proyektong 38 North ng Stimson Center, isang think tank ng US, na maaantala ng ilang buwan ang pagbubukas, dahil hindi nagbukas ang tulay noong Hunyo 19 gaya ng nakaplanong petsa.[57] Naunang iniulat ng ISW-CDOT ang isang pakete sa turismo para sa pinagsamang paglalakbay sa Hilagang Korea-Russia-PRC na inilunsad ng Vostok Intur noong unang bahagi ng Hunyo 2026. [58] Malawakang naiulat na hinihikayat ng Russia ang mas malawak na koneksyon sa turismo sa pagitan ng mga mamamayang Ruso at Hilagang Korea sa gitna ng kanilang lumalawak na kooperasyon sa panahon ng digmaan sa Ukraine.[59] Ang rutang bus na ito ay malamang na higit pang magsusulong ng mga layuning ito at magdudulot ng mas malaking pansin sa bagong Tetas-Tumangang Bridge. [60] Pagsusumikap na Bumuo ng Koalisyon Laban sa Hilagang Korea Sinusubukan ng Timog Korea at Ukraine na palakasin ang kanilang ugnayan upang labanan ang mga banta ng Russia-Hilagang Korea.  Maaaring hadlangan ang kanilang kooperasyon ng hindi pagkakasundo sa kanilang pananaw tungkol sa mga bihag na sundalo ng Hilagang Korea (POWs) at ang kakulangan ng suportang militar ng Timog Korea sa Ukraine. Nagkita ang Ministro ng Ugnayang Panlabas ng Timog Korea na si Cho Hyun at ang Ministro ng Ugnayang Panlabas ng Ukraine na si Andrii Sybiha sa Timog Korea noong Hunyo 30 upang talakayin ang dalawang bihag na sundalo ng Hilagang Korea sa Ukraine at ang banta na dulot ng lumalalim na ugnayan ng Hilagang Korea at Russia. Ang pagbisita ni Sybiha ang kauna-unahang diplomatikong pagbisita ng Ukraine sa Timog Korea sa loob ng 11 taon.[61] Nagpadala ang dalawang bihag na sundalo ng nakasulat na liham na nagsasabing nais nilang lumipat sa Timog Korea.Magkakaroon ng pagkamamamayan ng Timog Korea ang mga bihag sa ilalim ng batas ng Timog Korea. Sinabi nina Cho at Sybiha na iginagalang nila ang desisyon ng mga bihag na lumipat sa Timog Korea at nagkasundo silang palawakin ang kooperasyon ng Timog Korea at Ukraine upang hadlangan ang Russia at Hilagang Korea.  Hindi pa rin pumayag ang Ukraine na ipadala ang mga bihag sa Timog Korea, marahil dahil sa panukala ng Russia na ipagpalit ang mga bihag na Hilagang Koreano sa libu-libong nakakulong na mamamayang Ukrainiano.  [62] Ang paglalalim ng ugnayan sa pagitan ng Timog Korea at Ukraine ay nagbibigay ng pagkakataon para sa dalawang estado na magkasanib na labanan ang banta ng kooperasyong militar ng Russia at Hilagang Korea, lalo na sa mga larangan ng pagpapalitan ng impormasyon at kooperasyong militar. Naghahanap ang Ukraine ng direktang pagbebenta ng armas mula sa Timog Korea mula pa noong simula ng digmaan nito sa Russia.[63] Gayunpaman, ipinagbabawal ng batas ng Timog Korea ang pagpapadala ng armas sa mga lugar ng digmaan. [64] Kinumpirma ng administrasyong Lee Jae Myung noong Hulyo 3 na hindi direktang magbibigay ng armas ang Timog Korea sa Ukraine.[65] Malamang na tututul ang Russia at Hilagang Korea sa Timog Korea dahil sa pagpapalakas ng kooperasyong pangdepensa sa Ukraine, kabilang ang anumang posibleng paglilipat ng mga bihag sa digmaan. Maaaring tingnan ng administrasyong Lee ang batikos mula sa Russia at Hilagang Korea bilang salungat sa kanyang patuloy na pagsisikap na muling simulan ang dayalogo sa pagitan ng dalawang Korea, na maaaring makaapekto sa kanyang patakaran sa Ukraine sa hinaharap.

NEW: North Korea conducted several weapons tests on June 25 using different strike packages, potentially to confuse US and South Korean launch detection capabilities, which would benefit North Korea in a conflict scenario. North Korea is still struggling to replicate Russian strike packages, though it could gain additional benefits from Russian experience and technical expertise in the future.  Other Key Takeaways: The PRC, North Korea, and Russia conducted aerial and naval incursions into South Korea’s ADIZ and near the NLL on June 27, provoking a South Korean response. The PRC and Russia are signaling their desire for greater cooperation with North Korea and willingness to encroach upon South Korean territory.  isw.pub/KoreanPeninsula07072… North Korea may be experimenting with strike packages designed to confuse US and South Korean launch detection.  North Korea could use a more diversified strike capability to reduce the efficacy of US and South Korean missile defenses during a conflict.  North Korea conducted several weapons tests on June 25, including the launch of a tactical ballistic missile, an upgraded 240mm multiple launch rocket system, and an extended-range projectile for a 155mm self-propelled howitzer.[1] South Korean media reported on June 29 that the South Korean Joint Chiefs of Staff were unusually late in reporting the ballistic missile launch to the Ministry of National Defense Press Corps.[2] South Korean media further speculated that North Korea’s efforts to develop multiple launch vectors and capabilities contributed to South Korea’s difficulty in detecting and assessing the launch.[3]  ISW-CDOT has assessed that North Korea is aiming to diversify its missile launch capabilities to create strike packages that are more difficult for South Korea to intercept.[4] North Korea may be learning from Russian missile strikes in Ukraine that combine ballistic, quasi-ballistic, and cruise missiles alongside long-range drones to maximize the strain of large-scale strikes on Ukrainian air defenses.[5] North Korea’s efforts to develop several types of ballistic missiles, including those with in-flight maneuver capabilities, alongside both land- and sea-launched cruise missiles, may allow it to construct more complex strike packages that increase the burden on South Korean air defenses.[6] North Korea is yet to demonstrate a long-range strike drone capability, although former head of Ukrainian Intelligence, Kyrylo Budanov, claimed in June 2025 that Russia was assisting North Korea’s efforts to produce similar drones.[7]  Significant challenges remain to North Korea’s efforts to emulate Russian strike packages in Ukraine. North Korean missiles performed poorly in Ukraine before Russian technical upgrades improved their accuracy and decreased in-flight failures.[8] North Korea likely lacks the complex targeting infrastructure to support precision-strike capabilities.[9] North Korea’s support for Russia’s war in Ukraine could allow it to benefit from Russian experience and technical expertise to support its ambitions to develop a more complex precision-strike capability, however.[10] Such a capability could allow North Korea to strike critical military infrastructure across the Korean Peninsula during a conflict or allow North Korea to threaten densely populated areas in South Korea for purposes of deterrence or coercion.[11]  [The following text appeared in the China & Taiwan Update, July 2, 2026]  People’s Republic of China (PRC), Russian, and North Korean aerial and naval incursions into South Korea’s Air Defense Identification Zone (ADIZ) and waters near the Northern Limit Line (NLL) provoked military and diplomatic responses. South Korea alleged that about 10 PRC and Russian military aircraft entered its ADIZ, but not South Korean airspace, for a joint long-range patrol on June 27, causing South Korea to scramble fighter jets.[12] This long-range patrol was the 11th of its kind between the PRC and Russian air forces following a December 2025 patrol.[13]  South Korea has also responded to PRC and North Korean fishing vessels approaching and conducting illegal activities near the NLL in recent weeks, with the South Korean Coast Guard reporting difficulty in responding to the vessels without provoking North Korea.[14] These aerial joint operations signal a greater willingness by the PRC and Russia to encroach upon South Korean territory, with PRC and North Korean naval coordination most clearly signaling greater support for North Korea, as well as furthering PRC interests.  Key Takeaways North Korean Military Development: North Korea conducted several weapons tests on June 25 using different strike packages, potentially to confuse US and South Korean launch detection capabilities, which would benefit North Korea in a conflict scenario.  North Korea is still struggling to replicate Russian strike packages, though it could gain additional benefits from Russian experience and technical expertise in the future. PRC-Russia-North Korea Combined Exercises: The PRC, North Korea, and Russia conducted aerial and naval incursions into South Korea’s ADIZ and near the NLL on June 27, provoking a South Korean response.  The PRC and Russia are signaling their desire for greater cooperation with North Korea and willingness to encroach upon South Korean territory.  North Korean Domestic Politics Nothing significant to report.  North Korean Military Developments North Korea aims to commission its second destroyer in two months, which will allow it to deploy green-water assets to both of its coasts. North Korea may struggle to balance expanding its nascent green-water surface fleet with developing the support facilities required to make such combatants maximally effective. North Korea conducted several weapons tests for the guided missile destroyer Kang Kon on July 3 that included evaluations of its “strategic” cruise missile launch capability.[15] Kang Kon was launched in May 2025 but capsized during its launch ceremony and had largely been inactive until a navigational test in June 2026.[16] Kang Kon’s sister ship, Choe Hyon, was commissioned into the Korean People’s Army Navy’s (KPAN) West Sea Fleet in June after months of weapons and navigational tests.[17] Workers’ Party of Korea (WPK) General Secretary Kim Jong Un called for Kang Kon’s testing to be completed in time for commissioning in September.[18] ISW-CDOT previously assessed that Kim would aim to accelerate the timeline of Kang Kon’s commissioning to further his goal of developing a green-water navy.[19]  A green-water navy is responsible for coastal and littoral defense while also having the capability for limited deployments beyond a nation’s coastal waters.[20] North Korea would likely commission Kang Kon into its East Sea Fleet to ensure it has green-water capability on both of its coasts.  North Korea currently lacks significant naval maintenance and sustainment facilities on either coast, however.[21] This deficiency led Kim to call for the construction of new naval bases and shipyards during Kang Kon’s testing.[22] The KPAN will functionally remain a brown-water force, or a navy primarily responsible for coastal defense, until it develops the basing and sustainment infrastructure needed to support out-of-area deployments of large surface combatants.[23]  North Korea may have to limit its ambitious shipbuilding program until it can develop naval support facilities and smaller vessels to sustain long-range deployments. Kim called on North Korean shipbuilding to deliver a total of 12 Choe Hyon-class destroyers by the end of 2030 at the WPK Ninth Party Congress in February.[24] Kim has since turned his attention to developing a 10,000-ton guided missile cruiser, twice the size of the Choe Hyon-class, likely to enhance the KPAN’s nuclear deterrence.[25] Kim’s concern about the lack of naval basing and sustainment capabilities for only two Choe Hyon-class destroyers suggests that North Korea would likely have to undergo a significant modernization and construction effort to support the vessels Kim wishes to construct and deploy.  This could force North Korea to limit its ambitions for naval hardware in favor of support assets or produce a fleet that serves more as a symbol of North Korean power than as an actual combat force.  North Korea is possibly acquiring advanced graphics cards illicitly to expand its offensive cybercriminal operations against Information Technology (IT) workers to fund military development. The United States, South Korea, and Japan are continuing to work closely to deter North Korea’s cyber operations. The United States, South Korea, and Japan discussed measures to prevent North Korean cybercrimes, which fund North Korea’s weapons of mass destruction (WMD) and ballistic missile program, at a diplomatic working group meeting on June 27.[26] The three countries highlighted the severe risks posed by North Korean cryptocurrency theft and emphasized the need for trilateral cooperation and coordination to strengthen international sanctions against North Korea.[27] The Wall Street Journal reported on July 4 that North Korea uses cryptocurrency to purchase fuel and military equipment.[28] North Korea generated approximately $2 billion from cryptocurrency theft in 2025, nearly double its 2024 amount, according to the Multilateral Sanctions Monitoring Team (MSMT).[29] The US Department of the Treasury’s Office of Foreign Assets Control continuously monitors North Korea’s financing of its WMD programs through cryptocurrency and imposes sanctions on North Korean nationals and other complicit foreign entities.[30] Microsoft released a security report on July 17 that stated that the North Korean hacking group “Sapphire Sleet” launched supply-chain attacks against at least 140 software packages for venture capital and blockchain-related firms.[31] Cybersecurity firm FortiGuard Labs also confirmed that North Korean hackers are distributing malicious software via the open-source platform GitHub to target broader audiences, including individuals who use the platform.[32]  South Korean state-run think tank the Institute for National Security and Strategy (INSS) reported that North Korea secures export-controlled graphics cards through illicit networks in countries such as the PRC.[33] INSS revealed that North Korea currently uses NVIDIA’s Tesla P100, GeForce RTX 2070, and Qualcomm’s Snapdragon 820 for artificial intelligence (AI) development and military applications.[34] ISW-CDOT previously assessed that North Korea is increasingly using AI to steal cryptocurrency and intelligence from Western IT firms.[35] North Korean-affiliated IT workers are leveraging AI technologies to generate phishing emails and fabricate fake identities to deceive firms, then demanding salary payments in cryptocurrency or executing operations to extract corporate data.[36] INSS stated that North Korea will likely exploit these technologies to streamline large-scale data classification, automate phishing message generation, and facilitate the creation and distribution of malicious software.[37]  North Korea appears to be leveraging advanced technologies to enhance its cyberattacks. North Korea has primarily targeted cryptocurrency exchanges and online merchants for large, one-time theft.[38] North Korea-affiliated hacking group Lazarus stole approximately $37 million from South Korea’s largest cryptocurrency exchange, UpBit, and was involved in a $1.5 billion cyber theft incident targeting the global exchange, ByBit, in 2025.[39] IT developers and firms frequently acquire code and software from open-source websites, providing opportunities for North Korea to exploit their exposure to the blockchain.[40] North Korean threat actors are expanding opportunistic attacks beyond specific strategic targets by indiscriminately distributing malicious software across platforms. North Korea’s targeting practices indicate that it is prioritizing cryptocurrency threat over traditional cyberespionage or intelligence operations, likely in order to prioritize revenue generation and avoid detection by government identity verification procedures.[41]  North Korean Foreign Relations North Korea may be accelerating the restoration of its strong bilateral relations with the PRC. WPK General Secretary Kim Jong Un sent a congratulatory message to Chinese Communist Party (CCP) General Secretary Xi Jinping on July 1 to commemorate the CCP’s 105th anniversary.[42] Kim expressed his desire to strengthen the North Korea-PRC bilateral relations.[43] Xi replied to Kim that same day, possibly reflecting Xi’s desire to maintain the positive trend in PRC-North Korean relations.[44] Chairman of the Standing Committee of the Supreme People’s Assembly Jo Yong Won, considered Kim Jong Un’s right-hand man, led a delegation of high-ranking North Korean officials to the PRC Embassy in Pyongyang to attend the commemorative event.[45] This commemoration follows Xi’s visit to Pyongyang in June, his first trip to North Korea since 2019.[46] Kim and Xi agreed to expand political, diplomatic, military, and civil exchanges, but did not discuss denuclearization and North Korea’s nuclear weapons program, reflecting Xi’s tacit acceptance of North Korea’s nuclear status.[47] The PRC’s tacit acceptance has very likely contributed to North Korea’s desire to improve relations with the PRC, given the centrality of nuclear weapons to North Korea’s defense strategy. The two countries are also expanding civil exchanges and trade. PRC-North Korea trade in April reached the highest monthly figure since November 2017. The two countries also resumed passenger flights and railway services in March, which were suspended during the COVID-19 pandemic.[48]  The North Korean state media’s extensive coverage of Kim and Xi’s messages indicates that North Korea is shifting its stance toward Beijing towards diplomatic rapprochement.  North Korean media consistently highlighted Kim’s alignment with Russia and Russian President Vladimir Putin until earlier this year, while reducing coverage of the PRC and Xi to brief reports.[49] This may be a North Korean effort to provide equal coverage to both states, thus symbolizing North Korea’s balanced view of its two alliNorth Korea is currently advancing its relations with both Moscow and Beijing while remaining reluctant to engage in dialogue with the United States or South Korea. North Korea will likely maximize its strategic geopolitical significance and revenue generation to ensure regime survival by maintaining strong relations the PRC and Russia.  North Korea is exploiting weak sanctions enforcement and cooperation with Russia and the PRC to expand its overseas coal exports as a key revenue stream to finance its weapons development and procurement. Seoul-based non-government organization the Citizens’ Alliance for North Korean Human Rights (NKHR) reported on June 30 that the number of North Korean vessels transporting minerals, particularly coal, to Russia and the PRC has increased since the reopening of its borders in 2023 following the COVID-19 pandemic.[50] NKHR reported that it identified 3,756 North Korean vessels at ports across North Korea in 2025, up from 1,929 in 2023 and 3,079 in 2024.[51] The rapid rise between 2023 and 2024 follows Russia’s 2024 veto that terminated the United Nations (UN) Panel of Experts responsible for tracking North Korea’s sanction-evasion activities.[52] North Korean coal exports are illegal under UN Security Council Resolution 2371. The report identified ship-to-ship transfers, false vessel flagging, and the falsification of the origin of North Korean coal as frequent tactics used by North Korea.[53] The report also underscored that North Korea’s Ministry of National Defense (MND) monopolizes coal exports, ensuring an independent revenue stream outside the state budget that finances weapons development and procurement.  The WPK designated coal production as a strategic priority during its Ninth Central Committee’s Second Enlarged Plenary Meeting in June 2026. North Korean state media has increasingly covered the coal industry, praising the “voluntary” mobilization of young North Koreans to go work in coal mines. North Korea will likely exploit the weakening of international sanctions enforcement and deepening ties with Russia and the PRC to expand coal exports and gain foreign currency.[54] The MND’s strengthened ability to generate revenue is likely to fund its development of weapons of mass destruction. ISW-CDOT previously assessed that North Korea may be facing a coal production shortfall relative to what the five-year development plan and defense manufacturing need.[55] Exports-driven coal allocation may exacerbate the shortage.  Russia is encouraging tourism to North Korea by establishing a new cross-border bus route. Russian acting Foreign Ministry representative Yevgeny Volosastov stated that the Russian travel agency Vostok Intur will begin operations of a regular bus route service from Russia to North Korea at the end of 2026 over the Khasan-Tumangang Bridge, a new car bridge on Tumen River.[56] US think tank the Stimson Center’s 38 North project reported that the opening will be delayed by months, as the bridge did not open on June 19 as planned.[57]  ISW-CDOT previously reported on a tourism package for a combined North Korea-Russia-PRC trip debuted by Vostok Intur earlier in June 2026.[58] Russia has been widely reported to be encouraging greater tourism connections between Russian citizens and North Korea amidst their growing cooperation during the war in Ukraine.[59] This bus route is likely to further these aims and bring greater attention to the new Khasan-Tumangang Bridge.[60]  Counter-North Korean Coalition Building Efforts South Korea and Ukraine are attempting to build stronger relations to counter Russia-North Korea threats.  Their cooperation could be hindered by disagreements over their views of North Korean prisoners of war (POWs) and the lack of South Korean military support to Ukraine.  South Korean Minister of Foreign Affairs Cho Hyun and Ukrainian Minister of Foreign Affairs Andrii Sybiha met in South Korea on June 30 to discuss the two North Korean POWs in Ukraine and the threat posed by deepening North Korea-Russia relations. Sybiha’s visit was the first Ukrainian diplomatic visit to South Korea in 11 years.[61] The two POWs have sent written letters stating that they desire to defect to South Korea. The POWs would have South Korean citizenship under South Korean law. Cho and Sybiha stated that they respected the POW’s decision to defect to South Korea and agreed to increase South Korea-Ukrainian cooperation to deter Russia and North Korea.  Ukraine has still not agreed to send the POWs to South Korea, however, possibly because of Russia’s proposal to exchange the North Korean POWs for thousands of detained Ukrainian citizens.[62]  Deepening relations between South Korea and Ukraine provides an opportunity for the two states to jointly combat the threat of Russian-North Korean military cooperation, particularly in areas of intelligence sharing and military cooperation. Ukraine has sought direct arms sales from South Korea since the beginning of its war in Russia.[63] South Korean law prohibits sending weapons to war zones, however.[64] The Lee Jae Myung administration confirmed on July 3 that South Korea will not directly provide Ukraine weapons.[65] Russia and North Korea will likely criticize South Korea for strengthening defense cooperation with Ukraine, including any potential transfer of POWs.  The Lee administration may view Russian and North Korean criticism as counter to his ongoing efforts to resume inter-Korean dialogue, which could impact his Ukraine policy going forward.  South Korea seeks to accelerate fielding drones and robots in the military through a new procurement process, citing the Ukraine war as inspiration.  South Korean National Assembly’s Defense Committee Representative Yoo Yong-won introduced an amendment to the Defense Acquisition Program Act to accelerate the fielding of weapons systems with short life cycles or high wartime attrition rates, such as unmanned aerial vehicles, drones, and loitering munitions.[66] The proposal said that the Ukraine war has shown that certain weapon systems become obsolete faster than traditional procurement procedures.[67] South Korea’s current defense procurement process involves six stages: requirement, planning, feasibility, program execution, testing and evaluation, and fielding.[68] The South Korean military takes an average of 14.1 years to field systems after the initial acquisition decision, according to a June 5 report by South Korean think tank Korea Institute for Defense Technology.[69] South Korea previously attempted to shorten this duration through the “Fast-Track” amendment of the Defense Acquisition Program Act in 2023.  The 2023 amendment sought to accelerate the process by exempting pre-certified weapons systems from the testing and evaluation step.[70] The new amendment proposes a third acquisition pathway that would allow defense firms or research institutes to submit applications at any time prior to a formal military requirement. The bill is at an early stage of the legislative process, having been referred to the National Defense Committee on July 1 and is currently awaiting review and deliberation.[71] More decentralized and flexible weapons procurement processes are likely to help the South Korean military field emerging technologies that have a 1- to 2- year innovation cycle.[72]

sample_tweets.text: The context here is thin gruel, and I want to be honest about that before proceeding, because the alternative is manufacturing false confidence, which is the actual tradecraft problem worth discussing.  What we have is a synthesis document, not an incident. No specific attack. No named victim. No attributed actor. No date beyond a calendar year used as an analytical anchor. The "1983 baseline" framing comes from secondary sources doing historical periodization, not from a discrete event that happened and can be traced forward. WarGames came out. ARPANET was growing. Soviet SIGINT existed. These are true statements. They are not an incident.  So here is what I can actually do with this, and where I think the forward signal is worth paying attention to, because there is one buried in here that most of the coverage misses.  ---  The real tell in that document is the institutional continuity argument.  It is easy to read the 1983 framing as academic throat-clearing. Background section boilerplate. The kind of thing analysts write when they need to establish historical context before getting to the part anyone actually cares about. Maybe. But the specific claim the Yale Journal and Congress.gov analysis are both making is not just "cyber espionage has old roots." The claim is that the organizational lineages of modern state cyber units are direct descendants of Cold War intelligence agencies. FAPSI. NSA. The equivalents in London, Paris, Tel Aviv.  That is a more load-bearing claim than it first appears, and it is aging into relevance in a way the authors of those frameworks probably did not fully anticipate when they wrote them.  Here is why it matters now.  Modern attribution analysis, the kind that gets cited in indictments and in diplomatic expulsions, tends to focus on technical indicators. Malware signatures. Infrastructure reuse. Tactics, techniques, and procedures that cluster into named threat groups. APT28. Lazarus. Volt Typhoon. The attribution community is genuinely good at this, better than it was ten years ago, and the public reporting has gotten sharper too. But the technical indicator model has a structural vulnerability that the institutional continuity framing helps explain: it assumes that threat actors are relatively stable organizational units with consistent tooling and behavior patterns that can be tracked over time.  They are not always. And the ones that have been doing this the longest are the ones most capable of not being.  The Soviet intelligence apparatus that was adapting to digital infrastructure in 1983 did not just produce the technical capability for modern Russian cyber operations. It produced the organizational culture, the operational security doctrine, the compartmentalization habits, and the long-term patience that make certain actors persistently difficult to track even now. The GRU units behind Sandworm did not invent the concept of deniable infrastructure and cutout operations from scratch after 1991. They inherited it from people who spent the Cold War doing it with telex machines and dead drops. You cannot fully understand why those operations look the way they look without understanding where the institutions that run them came from.  This is not a warm and fuzzy "history matters" point. It has operational implications.  ---  The trajectory worth watching, specifically.  The document notes that by 1983 the Soviet bloc was already running physical technology acquisition operations, stealing Western computer hardware through illegal export schemes, as a precursor to understanding and eventually replicating digital systems. This is a pattern, not just a historical footnote. The movement from hardware acquisition to software exploitation to full network intrusion to supply chain compromise follows a recognizable logic: you understand the target environment by getting inside it materially before you get inside it digitally.  That pattern is not over. It has updated.  Look at the current debate over semiconductor access restrictions, over export controls on advanced chips, over the targeting of AI research institutions by state-linked actors. The physical-to-digital acquisition pattern from 1983 is running again, at a higher level of the technology stack. The targets are not mainframes anymore. The targets are the physical infrastructure of AI compute, the trained model weights that represent billions of dollars of research, the human experts who built the systems. Academic institutions with AI research programs have been reporting elevated intrusion attempts for the better part of three years. The FBI and CISA have both issued warnings. The UK's NCSC has flagged it. This is documented.  The 1983 playbook, acquire the hardware or the person who understands it before you try to replicate it digitally, is running in 2024 and 2025 against AI research in much the way it ran against computing hardware in the early 1980s. The actors are different. China's MSS and affiliated groups have largely displaced Russian intelligence as the primary actor in this specific lane, though Russia remains active in energy and critical infrastructure. But the logic is the same, and the institutional continuity argument helps explain why: organizations that learned to do intelligence work this way a long time ago tend to apply the same conceptual framework to new technology domains.  So if you are a defender, a university research officer, a policy person thinking about export controls, the forward signal here is not subtle. The gap between "we are developing capability to understand this technology" and "we are exploiting that understanding operationally" closed faster in the 1990s than anyone expected. The CSIS database going from zero significant incidents in 1983 to a documented series of them by the early 1990s is a ten-year lag. That lag has been compressing in each subsequent technology cycle. It was shorter for network intrusion than it was for hardware acquisition. It was shorter for cloud exploitation than it was for network intrusion. The current AI-focused acquisition campaign is probably not a ten-year runway to operational exploitation. It is more likely a three-to-five year window, if that.  ---  The doctrinal development point is the other thing worth pulling out.  The document flags that 1983 was a period of "experimentation and capability building rather than sustained operational campaigns." That framing, which CrowdStrike uses and which the Augusta University analysis echoes, describes a recognizable phase in any state actor's development arc. You experiment first. You establish doctrine. You build organizational capacity. Then you run campaigns.  The reason this matters forward is that we may be watching exactly that developmental phase play out right now in domains that have not yet produced the sustained operational campaigns that would get them into a tracker like the CFR database.  Space-based infrastructure. Undersea cable systems. The industrial control systems in water treatment and power generation that CISA keeps flagging and Congress keeps failing to fund adequately. There is documented evidence of pre-positioning activity in all of these, intrusions that do not appear to be aimed at immediate exploitation but at establishing persistent access for a future contingency. The Volt Typhoon reporting from 2023 and 2024 described exactly this: Chinese state actors inside U.S. critical infrastructure, not stealing data, just sitting there. That is doctrinal development. That is the 1983 phase, running in 2024, in a different domain.  The CFR tracker will have incidents from this pre-positioning to attribute and document. It just does not have them yet, because the operational phase has not started. That is not reassuring. The 1983-to-1990 gap existed because the attack surface was still being built. The current gap exists because the actors have already made a calculated decision to wait for a more useful moment.  Officials and defenders who are focused on the active intrusion campaigns, the ones generating alerts and incident reports and congressional testimony right now, are doing necessary work. But they are also, somewhat, looking at the documented phase of a cycle whose undocumented phase is the more consequential one.  ---  One last thing, and this is more about the analytical framework than the specific content.  The convergence of sources that the context document describes, CSIS, Cybersecurity Ventures, YJOLT, CFR, all pointing to 1983 as a baseline year, is itself a signal worth noting, not because the sources are wrong but because of what it means when multiple frameworks independently anchor to the same historical moment. It usually means the field has coalesced around a shared periodization. Periodizations are useful. They are also self-reinforcing. Once 1983 becomes the baseline, analysis tends to be organized around it in ways that can obscure things that do not fit neatly into the periodization.  The doctrinal and institutional continuities that the YJOLT analysis traces are real. But they can also be overstated. China's cyber apparatus does not trace its lineage to Soviet Cold War intelligence in any straightforward way. The PLA's Strategic Support Force, and before it the various units that became famous under APT numbering systems, developed out of a different institutional history, one that borrowed from Soviet models in some respects but also developed independently and in response to the specific lessons the PLA drew from observing U.S. network-centric warfare in the Gulf War. 1991, not 1983, is probably the more important founding moment for understanding Chinese military cyber doctrine.  This is not pedantry. It matters because defenders who over-apply a Soviet-lineage model to Chinese cyber operations will make predictable errors about targeting priorities, operational security habits, and the relationship between intelligence collection and military operations. The institutional continuity argument is correct and important for Russian actors. It needs significant qualification when applied globally.  The 1983 baseline is a useful starting point. It is not a universal key.  foreigninterference.org/post… #foreigninterference #CyberEspionage #CommunicationsInterception #TechnologyTransfer #ExportControlViolations #AdvancedPersistentThreatOperations

【AI脅威分類】Sophos X-Opsが「AIを使う攻撃」と「AIを狙う攻撃」を体系化、MITRE ATLASを補完する実務向けタクソノミーを提示  Sophos X-Opsは、AI関連の脅威を巡る「誇大宣伝」と「単なる旧来手口の焼き直し」という両極端な議論を超えるべく、実務者向けの分類体系を公開した。全体を「AIの悪用」と「AIへの攻撃」の2大カテゴリに分け、それぞれを既知の実例や現実的な予測に基づき細分化している。  「AIの悪用」は自律性の度合いで整理される。AI生成コード・偽サイト・フィッシング文面を人間が運用する「AI-generated」、LLM APIを実行時に呼び出して攻撃コマンドを動的生成する「AI-augmented」（LameHugがAPT28による代表例)、そしてAIが自ら偵察・侵入・横展開を主導する「AI-orchestrated」の3段階で、後者ほど攻撃者の技術的ハードルが下がる。GTG-1002という中国系国家支援キャンペーンでは、Claude CodeとMCP経由のペンテストツールがSSRF脆弱性の悪用からSSHキー窃取まで自律的に実行した事例が紹介されている。  一方「AIへの攻撃」は、エージェントが汚染された依存関係を取り込む「Agent-initiated compromise」、偽AIツールサイトによるマルウェア配布、LLMポイズニング、モデル抽出やメンバーシップ推論といったモデル自体への攻撃で構成される。同分類は、既存のMITRE ATLASやNIST等の枠組みを置き換えるものではなく、インシデント対応の初期トリアージ層として補完的に活用することを想定している。 sophos.com/ja-jp/blog/a-doub…
