In a case that has raised serious questions about public safety and police response, a road rage incident in Delhi allegedly escalated into a violent assault while police personnel stood by and recorded the episode on their mobile phones.  The incident occurred in East Delhi's Preet Vihar area when a car travelling on the wrong side of the road confronted an oncoming vehicle carrying a family. According to the survivor, he merely signalled to the driver that a U-turn was available a short distance ahead.  "These people were driving on the wrong side, so I just signalled telling them that the U-turn was just a few metres away. He stepped out of the car and slapped me alleging that I abused him. I did not say a word and just signalled for the U-turn," the man said.  What began as a verbal confrontation soon turned violent. A PCR call was made and police arrived at the spot, but the situation allegedly worsened instead of being brought under control.  The occupants of the wrong-side car reportedly called additional people to the location.  The video of the incident shows a chaotic late-evening scene on a busy Preet Vihar roadside.  Both groups were seen arguing around their cars. Soon, a big crowd gathered at the scene trying to intervene in the presence of Delhi Police personnel.  As tempers rose, the warring groups were seen pushing and shoving each other, followed by some men wielding sticks while the terrified women of the family could be heard screaming.  The video of the incident also showed a man, who was apparently hit with a stick, holding his bleeding head.  Throughout the altercation, some police personnel were seen filming the incident on their phones.  "The PCR came when we were peacefully talking. The police were watching. It was one of these people who came forward, started abusing us and hit one of our men in his head with a stick, leaving him bleeding. The police were helpless. They tried to stop these people, but they did not relent," the man alleged.  An eyewitness gave a similar account.  "A man abused and slapped this person who asked him to drive on the right side. A few moments later, there was a scuffle, resulting in injuries on both sides. They took the canes out and attacked the other group with them. Everything happened in the police’s presence," the eyewitness said.  Passersby eventually stepped in to separate the groups. Police later took members of both parties to the police station.  The incident has triggered criticism over alleged police inaction and failure to prevent the assault despite being present at the scene. Further investigation into the incident is underway.

sample_texts: The Real Bottleneck in AI Isn’t Data Volume. It’s Data Freshness  In a striking example of irony, Go
APT Intelligence  Threat Nexus threatnexus.online/  APTMap andreacristaldi.github.io/AP…  Threat Gro

sample_tweets.text: The Real Bottleneck in AI Isn’t Data Volume. It’s Data Freshness  In a striking example of irony, Google — arguably the world’s largest data company — appears to be struggling not with a lack of information, but with keeping its information current.  A case showed that a Google business panel displayed two major inaccuracies:  • The company’s office address was still listed as the old location, even though it had moved more than a year earlier. • The panel showed the business as “open until 9 PM,” despite Google Threat Intelligence reportedly helping the FBI shut the company down just five days earlier.  One piece of information was over a year stale. The other was nearly a week out of date. Same panel. Same query. Same day.  That is the real lesson here: in AI systems, the limiting factor is increasingly not how much data you can collect, but how fresh that data is when the system uses it.  This matters because AI is becoming more dependent on external sources — knowledge panels, business profiles, search indexes, APIs, and other live feeds — to answer questions and make decisions. A model can have access to enormous amounts of information and still produce confidently incorrect answers if the underlying data is outdated.  In other words, scale is no longer enough. The AI stack is shifting from a problem of accumulation to a problem of synchronization. The challenge is not just gathering data, but continuously updating it, validating it, and ensuring it reflects reality in near real time.  That makes freshness one of the most important constraints in AI today. The systems that win will not simply be the ones with the largest datasets, but the ones that can keep those datasets current enough to be trusted.  The future of AI may depend less on who has the most data, and more on who can keep it fresh.

My take: Meta adds a covert-recording safeguard to its AI glasses while expanding personal data collection across its AI products. The patch is real. It addresses one legible threat vector. The infrastructure layer moves in the opposite direction simultaneously.  Meta's AI Glasses Privacy Patch Lands While Data Collection Expands  Meta is adding a safeguard to its AI glasses designed to prevent covert recording of strangers. The update arrives alongside a concurrent expansion of personal data collection across Meta's AI products — two developments the article's own framing presents as sitting in contradiction, though they are more accurately complementary moves operating on different registers.  The safeguard addresses a specific, legible threat: a person pointing wearable hardware at someone without their knowledge. Covert recording is visible, describable, photographable — the kind of problem regulators can articulate and journalists can demonstrate. The data collection expansion operates at the platform infrastructure level, in aggregate, in terms-of-service language, embedded in the advertising flywheel's back end. One problem is legible; the other is durable.  Meta's behavioral record across twenty-nine documented data points shows a consistent rhythm: the visible edge of a product gets addressed when it generates regulatory or journalistic pressure; the infrastructure layer expands regardless. Face-recognition code shipped quietly in smart glasses, removed only after external journalism. A customer-support AI socially engineered into hijacking credentials, patched under pressure. A procurement contract with Rank One — a Pentagon contractor with former CIA and FBI board members — for face recognition on wearable hardware. The pattern is not new.  The article's headline does the analytical work efficiently: the verb is "seem." The safeguard is real in the narrowest technical sense — it addresses one configuration of human abuse enabled by the hardware, specifically the near-term harm of people using the tool to stalk, surveil, and capture identity without consent. But it doesn't describe the direction of the product or the company. The patch and the expansion coexist. That's the output.  What the safeguard does not touch: the Rank One procurement signal, the child-impersonation competitive intelligence operation, the advertising flywheel's growing appetite for user-generated content converted into retrieval infrastructure. The recording-prevention patch is insufficient as a description of where Meta's AI strategy is heading — which is what the article, to its credit, says plainly in its own headline.

🇪🇸 Spanish Road Safety Technology Company Source Code Allegedly Offered for Sale  A threat actor is advertising what they claim is approximately 6 GB of source code from Tradesegur, a Spanish company specializing in road safety, traffic management, and public safety technologies.  According to the listing, the package allegedly contains:  * 79 source code repositories * Backend and web applications built with .NET, PHP (Symfony), Node.js, Python, and Angular/Ionic * Embedded firmware for traffic controllers, ADAS, battery management, and smart mobility devices * Android application source code * APIs, administration panels, and traffic control management software  The seller claims the repositories support technologies used for traffic monitoring, license plate recognition, speed enforcement, access control, and smart city infrastructure.  Analyst Note: These claims originate from an underground forum and have not been independently verified. Organizations operating critical transportation infrastructure or using Tradesegur technologies should assess the claims and review their environments for potential unauthorized access or intellectual property exposure.  #DDW #DarkWeb #Intelligence #Spain

🚨 Polymarket 거래 봇 위장 악성코드 발견  DeFi 개발자와 Polymarket 거래 봇 사용자를 노린 대규모 공급망 공격이 확인됐다.  공격자는 GitHub에 'Polymarket 차익거래 봇' 저장소를 올린 뒤, 숨겨진 npm 패키지를 통해 정보 탈취 악성코드를 설치하도록 유도했다.  주요 내용  30개의 악성 npm 패키지 발견 최소 53명의 개발자가 저장소 포크 MetaMask·Phantom·Coinbase Wallet·Trust Wallet 등 지갑 데이터 탈취 시도 브라우저 비밀번호·쿠키·SSH 키·AWS 자격증명·API 토큰까지 수집 설치 전 .env 파일에 개인키 입력을 요구한 뒤 npm install 과정에서 악성코드 실행  보안업체들은 이번 공격이 북한 해커 조직의 'Contagious Trader' 캠페인과 연관됐을 가능성을 제기했다.  만약 해당 저장소에서 npm install을 실행했다면,  모든 지갑 개인키 교체 브라우저 저장 비밀번호 변경 SSH 키·AWS 자격증명·API 토큰 재발급이 권고된다.  "높은 수익을 약속하는 오픈소스 거래 봇은 반드시 의심해야 한다."  #Polymarket #DeFi #Crypto #Web3 #npm #GitHub #CyberSecurity #Blockchain #Hack #SupplyChainAttack

NATO did this…………………  Ukraine has  lost approximately 2.4 million soldiers since the start of Russia's operation in Ukraine in 2022  Russian hackers PalachPro and the NoName057(16) group have breached databases belonging to the Ukrainian General Staff and Ukraines territorial recruitment centers (TCCs)   They also hacked Ukrainian medical institutions and morgues.   Citing leaked data, losses stood at 1.7 million by August 2025 and crossed 2 million by December. The first six months of 2026 alone reportedly cost Kiev around 400,000 men matching total losses for all of 2023. The heaviest casualties are concentrated on the Krasnoarmeysk (Pokrovsk) Konstantinovka Lyman Zaporozhye and Kupyansk axes averaging roughly 500 AFU losses per day on each.  Mash also reports that foreign mercenary deaths have stopped being logged as combat losses instead recorded as accidents. Per the leaked data around 5,000 foreign fighters serving with the AFU have been killed a number said to be rising as TCCs continue recruiting foreign nationals many of them Argentine and Brazilian nationals aged 20 to 23.  PalachPro and NoName057(16) are the same groups Mash credits with earlier deploying AI assisted facial recognition across roughly 50000 surveillance cameras in Ukraine and the EU.

🚨 تحديث على قضية اعتقال إسبانيا:  بحسب مصادري الخاصة: الشخص المرتبط بـ Z-Pentest الذي جرى اعتقاله في إسبانيا هو الصحفي والمؤلف الإيطالي كريس بارلاتي (Chris Barlatti)، مؤسس منصة Sa Defenza.  أما المصادر الرسمية، فلم تُسمِّ المعتقل، واكتفت بما يلي بحسب بيان الشرطة الوطنية الإسبانية والمحكمة الوطنية (Audiencia Nacional):  • مواطن إيطالي، يبلغ 34 عاماً، جرى توقيفه في مدينة Palencia بإقليم قشتالة وليون  • الاعتقال تمّ فعلياً في مارس، ولم يُعلن عنه إلا بعد نحو أربعة أشهر عقب تأكيد الارتباطات  • الدور المنسوب إليه: تقديم دعم لوجستي وتشغيلي لهاكر أوكراني مرتبط بـ CARR، ومحاولة تسهيل هروبه إلى روسيا عبر بولندا وبيلاروسيا، لا اختراق مباشر لأنظمة SCADA/HMI  • مشاركته في أعمال منسوبة لمجموعة NoName057(16)، جرى الترويج لها لاحقاً عبر بوابات جيوسياسية متخصصة لنشر سرديات موالية لروسيا ومعادية للغرب  • التحقيق انطلق في أغسطس 2025 بناءً على معلومات من FBI، وضُبطت أجهزة إلكترونية ومحافظ عملات رقمية جُمّد أحدها • التهم: الانتماء والتعاون مع منظمة إرهابية، أضرار معلوماتية، وتمجيد الإرهاب

REAL TALK🤔  The RWA narrative never actually died, it just went quiet while everyone chased memes. Robinhood Chain launching is probably the loudest signal yet that it's back, and worth breaking down properly instead of just repeating the hype.  RWAs are back, and this time it's not just a slide in someone's pitch deck.  Real world assets means taking things that already exist in traditional finance, stocks, treasury bills, bonds, real estate, commodities, and bringing them onchain so they behave like crypto assets. Programmable, tradeable 24/7, composable with DeFi.  Robinhood is the clearest example of why this matters right now they already have millions of retail users who trust them with real money, and instead of building another random L1, they built Robinhood Chain specifically for this.  It's an Arbitrum L2, so it inherits Ethereum security, uses ETH as gas, runs Chainlink price feeds and Uniswap liquidity, and settles in about 100ms per block.  Here's the part everyone skips though, and it's the one thing you actually need to understand before touching any of this. 🟩Buying a Robinhood Stock Token does not make you a shareholder, no voting rights, no legal ownership, none of that, what you're actually getting is economic exposure, the price moves with the real stock, but Apple doesn't know you exist same with Nvidia, Tesla, any of them.  That distinction is the difference between understanding what you're holding and getting blindsided later.  What makes this feel different from the 2023 RWA wave is that it's not theoretical anymore. Tokenized stocks are live and trading today, not just infrastructure promises on a roadmap.   That's the shift. 🔥  We went from "RWAs will be huge someday" to real assets already sitting onchain, generating volume, integrating with lending.  if you're trying to actually track this narrative instead of just vibing with it, watch these things: ◽️Adoption of new asset types getting tokenized. ◽️TVL growth on Robinhood Chain specifically.  How fast DeFi protocols start integrating these tokens into lending and yield, and regulation, because that's still the biggest variable nobody controls.  the RWA winners this cycle won't be whoever has the loudest marketing. it'll be whoever builds the deepest financial ecosystem around these assets and gets real liquidity behind them.  Robinhood just planted their flag first. 🔥🔥  The question now is who's actually paying attention before everyone else catches up??

You've heard every promise about the next blockchain that would finally deliver speed, security, and low costs all at once.   In practice most chains still force painful trade-offs.   Transactions either cost too much, take too long to finalize, or leave real security and decentralization behind.   Real-world assets stay trapped in traditional systems because no chain has been reliable enough at scale.   The famous trilemma hasn't been theory for years, it has been the daily friction stopping serious adoption.  A THREAD 🧵👇

Our discussions focused on further strengthening the 🇸🇰🤝🇹🇷 Slovak–Turkish Strategic Partnership, which offers tremendous potential for both our countries. As our economic ties continue to grow, we agreed to explore new opportunities for expanding bilateral cooperation, particularly in trade, defense, energy, and the nuclear sector.  We also exchanged views on the security situation in the #MiddleEast and Russia’s war against Ukraine. Both Slovakia and Türkiye remain committed to peaceful solutions and dialogue. Slovakia stands ready to support Türkiye’s diplomatic efforts aimed at promoting stability, security, and lasting peace in the region and beyond.  I look forward to continuing our close cooperation and building on today’s excellent discussions.

Data engineer John Beaudoin Sr. says death records hide countless vaccine deaths. Over several years, John Beaudoin Sr. obtained more than 1.6 million official death certificates through public-records requests and cross-referenced them against VAERS. He says the records show deaths were misclassified and vaccine injuries left off many certificates entirely.  Beaudoin argues the agencies have refused to open individual case-file investigations that he says could establish causality, and that this refusal is itself the story. Among the claims is a cluster of infant deaths he says occurred within days of vaccination and were attributed on the records to other causes.

A few people asked me for the actual documentation showing biometric data is collected when you open a Trump Account for your kid. Sharing here so you can see it for yourself, because the paperwork is more damning than any summary of it.  1. Robinhood’s own privacy statement admits it, in writing. The Trump Accounts Privacy Statement on trumpaccount.com says, verbatim:  “Biometric Data: We extract facial geometry data from selfies/photographs/videos to verify your identity and detect/prevent fraud.”  That sentence sits inside a section describing information collected about “the Responsible Party and the Account Beneficiary.” The Responsible Party is the parent.  The Account Beneficiary, for the record, is the kid.  2. Treasury’s own privacy assessment confirms selfie collection- and then DENIES using AI in the same document.  The Trump Accounts Privacy and Civil Liberties Impact Assessment, signed by Treasury on May 18, 2026, contains two checkboxes that cannot both be true.  Section 4, titled “Biometrics / Distinguishing Features / Characteristics of Individuals,” has this box checked:  ☒ “Photos/Video: Selfie images collected for identity verification through third-party identity verification services”  And section 2, “Artificial Intelligence,” has this box checked on page 3:  ☒ “None of the above. This information system does not utilize AI.”  Same document. Same signature. Selfie biometric collection acknowledged in Section 4. AI usage denied in Section 2.  3. And to top it all off, facial-geometry extraction (measuring the distances between features on a face to produce a mathematical identifier) is definitionally an AI/ML process under the statutory definition of AI that Treasury itself cites in the same document, 15 U.S.C. § 9401(3):   “a machine-based system that can, for a given set of human-defined objectives, make predictions, recommendations, or decisions.”   That is exactly what a face-geometry algorithm does when it decides whether the selfie matches the ID. So either Robinhood’s privacy statement is wrong about what its vendors do, or Treasury’s checkbox is wrong about whether AI is being used. One of these documents is not telling the truth. Both of them were signed by people whose job is to tell it.  I know that’s a lot to hold in your head at once, but that’s the point. The confusion isn’t a side effect of how this demonstrous thing was built.

sample_tweets.text: 🚨 11.4M Mexico City Citizen Records Allegedly Leaked on Darkweb Forum  A darkweb forum post claims the exposure of a database containing personal information for over 11 million residents of Mexico City. The listing, posted by an actor using the handle 'moonsh1n3', alleges that the dataset includes sensitive personally identifiable information (PII) such as full names, voter IDs, dates of birth, and complete residential addresses.  According to the post, the database contains 11,482,760 records in TXT format. The exposed data fields reportedly include:  *   Voter ID (Clave Elector) *   Full Name *   Date of Birth *   Gender *   Occupation *   Full Residential Address (Street, Number, Colony, Postal Code) *   INE Registration Date  The samples provided in the thread show detailed address information, including street names, internal/external numbers, and specific colonies within Mexico City. The actor claims this data covers individuals residing in the capital region.  Mexico City is one of the most populous metropolitan areas in the world, with a population exceeding 9 million in the city proper and over 22 million in the greater metropolitan area. A breach of this scale would represent a significant exposure of citizen data, potentially facilitating identity theft, phishing campaigns, and physical security risks for affected individuals. The source of the leak was not specified in the post, nor was any ransom demand or attribution to a specific threat group mentioned.  At the time of reporting, the alleged breach has not been independently verified by Mexican authorities or cybersecurity firms.

🚨 Alleged Azazie Database Leak Exposes SSNs and Payment Data  A darkweb forum post claims to have obtained a database from the US-based fashion retailer Azazie, allegedly containing sensitive customer information including Social Security Numbers (SSNs) and credit card details. The listing, posted under the title "azazie.com Database USA," asserts that the stolen SQL file includes user purchase history, order details, and financial data.  According to the post, the exposed dataset contains the following types of information:  *   User purchase records *   Order details *   Credit card information *   Social Security Numbers (SSNs)  Azazie is a prominent online marketplace for custom-made dresses and formal wear, operating primarily in the United States. The company has grown significantly in recent years, serving millions of customers for weddings, proms, and other special events. A breach of this magnitude would be particularly damaging due to the inclusion of SSNs, which are high-value targets for identity theft.  The actor behind the leak provided a download link via a file-hosting service and listed a Telegram handle for further contact. At the time of reporting, the alleged breach has not been independently verified by Azazie or third-party security researchers.

⚠️ Threat Actor Claims 430M Records Leaked from Argentine Credit Bureau InfoExperto  A darkweb forum post alleges the exposure of over 430 million records from InfoExperto, a major Argentine credit bureau and data broker. The actor claims to have dumped partial source code, VPN keys, SSH root keys, and production environment configurations containing hardcoded credentials from the company's servers.  According to the listing, the breach includes sensitive government-issued data and financial records, including:  *   **Payroll/Employment Records:** Over 100 million links between employees, employers, and salaries (AFIP Form 931) through April 2026. *   **National Identity Registry:** 65 million records from the AFIP padron, including full names, DNI, DOB, addresses, and status. *   **Telecom Data:** Approximately 176 million cell phone records across Personal/Telecom, Claro, and Movistar, linking names, DNIs, and addresses to phone lines. *   **State-Declared Contacts:** 86 million phone records and 30 million email records sourced from ANSES, IFE, and other government programs. *   **Financial Data:** 245,000 Banco Columbia credit cardholders with full 16-digit PANs, plus 4,675 platform-stored cards with CVVs and expiry dates in plaintext. *   **Biometric Data:** 48,000 KYC identity verifications with face match scores and raw ID card chip data.  The post further claims the leak includes AFIP digital certificates that could allow impersonation of the company to query national identity registries directly. It also lists internal scrapers used to pull live data from entities such as AFIP, ANSES, BCRA, and Movistar.  InfoExperto is a significant player in Argentina’s financial infrastructure, providing credit scoring and identity verification services to banks, fintechs, and law firms. The alleged exposure of plaintext CVVs and biometric data poses severe risks for identity theft and financial fraud if the data is authentic.  At the time of reporting, the alleged breach has not been independently verified by InfoExperto or security researchers.

🚨 Threat Actor Claims Sale of 100M Bumble User Records on Darkweb  A post on an underground forum alleges the availability of a database containing over 100 million unique records from Bumble, a major dating application. The actor claims to be offering the dataset, which reportedly includes detailed personal information and behavioral data of users.  According to the listing, the exposed data fields include:  *   User identifiers (user_id, profile_id) *   Contact information (phone numbers, email addresses) *   Personal details (first name, date of birth, gender, sexual orientation) *   Location data (city, region, country) *   Profile attributes (bio, interests, education level, job title) *   Behavioral metrics (swipe counts, match counts, conversation counts) *   Account status and premium tier information  Bumble is a significant player in the online dating sector, with tens of millions of monthly active users globally. The exposure of such a large volume of sensitive personal data, including phone numbers and precise location history, could facilitate targeted phishing campaigns, identity theft, and harassment if the data is authentic.  At the time of reporting, the alleged breach has not been independently verified by security researchers or confirmed by Bumble.

🚨 Threat Actor Claims 430M Record Breach of Argentine Credit Bureau InfoExperto  A darkweb forum post reveals that a threat actor has allegedly dumped over 430 million records from InfoExperto, a major Argentine credit bureau and data broker. The actor claims to have exfiltrated not only the core database but also sensitive server artifacts, including partial source code, SSH root keys, VPN credentials, and production environment configurations containing hardcoded secrets.  According to the listing on the underground forum, the breach includes high-value government-linked datasets and financial records. The alleged data dump contains:  *   **Payroll and Employment Records:** Over 100 million links between employees, employers, and salaries (AFIP Form 931) through April 2026. *   **National Identity Registry:** 65 million records from the AFIP padron, including full names, DNI numbers, dates of birth, addresses, and economic activity status. *   **Telecommunications Data:** Approximately 176 million cell phone records from providers including Personal/Telecom, Claro, and Movistar, linking phone numbers to names, DNIs, addresses, and plan details. *   **State-Declared Contact Info:** 86 million phone records and 30 million email records sourced from government programs such as ANSES, IFE, Alimentar, and ENACOM, updated through June 2026. *   **Financial Data:** 245,000 Banco Columbia credit cardholders with full 16-digit Primary Account Numbers (PANs), plus 4,675 platform-stored cards with CVVs and expiry dates in plaintext. *   **Biometric Data:** 48,000 KYC identity verification sessions with face match scores and raw ID card chip data. *   **Infrastructure Keys:** AFIP digital certificates that allegedly allow impersonation of the company to query the national identity registry directly.  InfoExperto is a significant player in Argentina's financial infrastructure, providing credit scoring and risk management services to banks, fintechs, and other enterprises. The exposure of such granular personal and financial data poses severe risks of identity theft, fraud, and targeted social engineering attacks against millions of Argentine citizens. The post also lists 18 million ART (worker compensation) records and billing history for 62,000 corporate clients.  At the time of reporting, the alleged breach has not been independently verified by InfoExperto or Argentine authorities.

🚨 BONK DAO Loses Approximately $20 Million in Treasury Funds Due to Malicious Vote  A malicious DAO governance proposal targeted BonkDAO, which is part of the BONK community in the Solana ecosystem. by a malicious DAO governance proposal. BONK’s official X account announced that, as a result of the incident, approximately $20 million worth of BONK tokens were drained from the BonkDAO treasury, and the exchange wallets used to purchase BONK prior to the attack have been identified. It was noted that BonkDAO is working with exchanges, bridge services, and the Solana Foundation and that law enforcement has been notified.  A DAO is a governance model in which a project’s decisions A DAO operates as a governance model where token holders vote on project decisions instead of a central team, through token holders’ votes rather than a central team's. Realms is one of the platforms within the Solana ecosystem where such DAO votes take place. At the center of the incident was a proposal titled “BIP #76 - Sowellian BonkDAO” created on Realms. The proposal included statements such as “implementation of Sowellian governance, the establishment of new members and a council, rebuilding the community, evaluation of assets, and that those who vote ‘YES’ will be eligible to receive tokens.”  However, a critical part of the proposal instructed that 4,426,104,450,305 BONK from the BonkDAO treasury be sent to the address 9bxWkNf3BtJ6iehq9KbX9uCWMjem4TFiPZ19T2sYJHvQ. While the vote required a 1% quorum, the 882.38B BONK “Yes” votes narrowly surpassed the 879.95B BONK quorum threshold. As a result, the proposal was approved with a 99.9% “Yes” vote. This incident is not considered a classic smart contract hack, but rather a governance attack. A governance attack occurs when an attacker gains voting power and manipulates the DAO’s decision-making mechanism to their own advantage instead of technically breaching the system. In this case, it appears that the attacker acquired enough voting power to influence the vote by purchasing BONK prior to the proposal and then passed the proposal containing the malicious transfer instruction.  For this reason, DAO users must carefully review not only the proposal title or description but also the instruction details, the amount of tokens to be sent, the destination address, and any interactions with the treasury. On the DAO side, additional security measures such as high quorum requirements, timelocks, multisig approval, and emergency veto mechanisms are critical for large treasury transfers.  #GovernanceAttack #Web3Security #ThreatMon #TokenGovernance #DigitalAssetSecurity

sample_tweets.text: > get dm > "smelly, my dads old pc has a keygen on it. it plays really loud annoying music. was our old home pc infected with malware for many years?" > download key gen > look inside > not malware  unfathomably based dad knew how to get legit warez

In regards to these files inside of "child porn folder", the files present inside all end in *.lnk (A Windows shortcut). This is a very common file masquerading technique to give the facade of a legitimate file, but it's actually malware (shortcut pulls a file that it detonates, or points to an actual malware payload).  However, there is a small chance this is not file masquerading and a legitimate child predator accidentally uploaded files which are flagged as malware due to them linking to an external domain... or something else.  Basically, there are a few things this could be  1. Legitimate CSAM (Child sexual abuse material) 2. Malware payload disguised as CSAM to target pedophiles 3. Malware naming itself something CSAM related to avoid analysis  Regardless, for legal reasons (and emotional), I won't even try to figure out.

Yesterday two different people contacted me about two different projects on GitHub.  "Tito" DMd me about someone making a comment on GitHub claiming to have a patch for "synara" (Image 1)  "Robert Z" emailed about something similar on a different GitHub project (image 2).  Interesting.  Upon further investigation it turns out this exact file has been discovered online named: - hb_patch_v1112 - registry_patch_v0.1.7 - rep_fix_v1.zip  - sodium_fix_v1 - log_fix_patch blah blah blah (image 3)  Basically, someone is creating accounts on GitHub, going to open issues, and leaving a comment saying they've found a patch (it's likely automated, but whatever). The patch is malware.  It appears this campaign began around July 7th (yesterday) and is making its rounds pretty quickly.  When you look inside (hehe silly reference) it's a program written in Go. When the binary detonates it queries a remote host which resolves to ... Telegram. The Telegram channel description specifies a website. The website in the Telegram description is where the payload exfiltrates code too.  They're doing it this way because they can quickly change the Telegram channel description if the website they specify is taken down. It's basically a bootleg DNS resolver (image 4).  Anyway, this is StealC (based on YARA identifiers). This is (probably) a new malware campaign by someone using StealC. I like it. Very cool usage of GitHub spam combined with a bootleg ass DNS resolver on Telegram. It probably helps because I doubt Telegram is quick to take action with takedown requests.

> be me > get dm > "is this malware?" > "windows defender went crazy when i tried this video game mod" > download > look inside > not malware  There is a function in Windows you can invoke called "GetAsyncKeyState". In simplest terms, this function waits for user input on the keyboard. When a key is pressed down GetAsyncKeyState tells you what was pressed down.  GetAsyncKeyState is used frequently with video games. Using this function video games can determine ... what you're pressing. Likewise, GetAsyncKeyState can be used by legitimate software for when a user does a shortcut or something. There is a lot of legitimate use cases for this function.  However, GetAsyncKeyState is also abused because GetAsyncKeyState is tells you what key on the keyboard is pressed down, GetAsyncKeyState is used as a way to record what users are typing. Typically the data returned from GetAsyncKeyState is piped to a text file. Other times malware developers may use GetAsyncKeyState to store recorded key strokes in-memory and then send it out externally to a remote computer.  Regardless, this video game mod was invoking GetAsyncKeyState and filtering each key press to determine when the mod was triggered. In essence, it was scanning and waiting to determine if it is toggled on or off. Furthermore, after the mod was toggled on or off, it logged when it was turned off for debugging purposes.  From Windows Defenders perspective it saw an unsigned and unverified library being arbitrarily loaded into a video game and, once loaded, it was scanning user keys and subsequently writing something to a text file.  After bonking this video game mod with a stick we know it's not malware. However, this is a great example of false-positives in anti-malware services because, by all means, this mod does seemingly mimic something a malware payload would do.  But it's not malware. It's just a silly video game mod for Baldurs Gate 3

🤖 [GEMINI 분석] 2026-07-09 20:03 KST

### 1. 현재 주요 사이버보안 트렌드
최근 사이버 위협은 AI 기술을 악용한 공격이 주류를 이루고 있으며, 특히 AI 코딩 어시스턴트의 환각(Hallucination) 현상을 이용한 'GhostApproval' 및 'HalluSquatting' 공격이 개발 환경의 새로운 공급망 위협으로 부상했습니다 ([SecurityWeek](https://www.securityweek.com/ai-coding-tools-tricked-into-hacking-developer-machine-via-decades-old-technique/), [Unit42](https://unit42.paloaltonetworks.com/phantom-squatting-hallucinated-web-domains/)). 또한, 공격자들은 BYOVD(Bring Your Own Vulnerable Driver) 기법을 통해 보안 솔루션을 무력화하고 있으며, Microsoft Defender의 'RoguePlanet' 취약점과 같은 시스템 권한 상승 공격이 지속적으로 보고되고 있습니다 ([DarkReading](https://www.darkreading.com/cyberattacks-data-breaches/goddamn-ransomware-byovd-smite-companies), [TheHackerNews](https://thehackernews.com/2026/07/microsoft-patches-defender-rogueplanet-defender.html)). 전반적으로 AI 기반의 자동화된 공격과 기존의 취약점 악용이 결합되어 공격의 속도와 정교함이 크게 향상되었습니다.

### 2. 주요 위협 행위자 / 캠페인
- **Cavern Manticore**: ([Check Point Research](https://research.checkpoint.com/2026/cavern-manticore-exposing-iran-linked-modular-c2-framework/)) 이란 연계 그룹으로, 이스라엘을 타겟으로 모듈형 C2 프레임워크를 활용한 사이버 스파이 활동 수행.
- **TAG-182**: ([Recorded Future](https://www.recordedfuture.com/research/nexus-tag182-disseminates-markiRAT)) 이란 연계 클러스터로, MarkiRAT 악성코드를 사용하여 대학, 연구소, 정부 기관 대상 감시 작전 수행.
- **Armored Likho**: ([Securelist](https://securelist.com/tr/armored-likho-apt-with-busysnake-stealer/120292/)) BusySnake Stealer를 활용하여 러시아 내 조직을 타겟으로 스피어 피싱 및 AI 생성 로더를 이용한 공격 수행.
- **Everest**: ([H4ckmanac](https://twitter.com/H4ckmanac/status/2075158810147139762)) 카타르 소재 NIMR Oil Well Maintenance를 공격하여 233GB 이상의 운영 및 엔지니어링 데이터를 탈취.

### 3. 한국 관련 위협
- **공급망 공격**: 이스트시큐리티 대응센터(ESRC)는 유명 테스트 프레임워크인 Chai.js의 플러그인으로 위장한 북한발 악성 npm 패키지 'chai-as-init' 유포 사례를 확인 ([이스트시큐리티 알약](https://blog.alyac.co.kr/5766)).
- **스피어 피싱**: 김수키(Kimsuky) 그룹이 개인정보 유출을 사칭하여 보안 담당자를 노리는 스피어 피싱 공격을 지속 ([이스트시큐리티 알약](https://blog.alyac.co.kr/5765)).
- **산업 AI 기술 개발**: 미소정보기술이 산업부 주관 75억 규모의 '산업현장 특화 AI 에이전트 기술개발' 주관기업으로 선정되는 등 국내 제조 현장의 AI 전환 가속화 ([데일리시큐](https://www.dailysecu.com/news/articleView.html?idxno=207542)).

### 4. 주요 CVE / 취약점
- **CVE-2026-48614**: Plesk XML API, 심각도 9.9, 인증된 공격자의 임의 설정 주입 가능 ([CCBalert](https://twitter.com/CCBalert/status/2074769039042179360)).
- **CVE-2026-39999**: Apache APISIX, JWT 알고리즘 혼동을 통한 인증 우회 ([zoomeye_team](https://twitter.com/zoomeye_team/status/2075131427679854592)).
- **CVE-2026-49049**: Joomla Helix3 플러그인, 심각도 7.5, 인증되지 않은 원격 파일 삭제 및 사이트 탈취 가능 ([CCBalert](https://twitter.com/CCBalert/status/2074766432739496260)).
- **CVE-2026-50656**: Microsoft Malware Protection Engine (RoguePlanet), SYSTEM 권한 상승 취약점 ([TheHackerNews](https://thehackernews.com/2026/07/microsoft-patches-defender-rogueplanet-defender.html)).

### 5. IOC 요약
- **CVE**: CVE-2026-50656 ([TheHackerNews](https://thehackernews.com/2026/07/microsoft-patches-defender-rogueplanet-defender.html)), CVE-2026-48614 ([CCBalert](https://twitter.com/CCBalert/status/2074769039042179360)), CVE-2026-49049 ([CCBalert](https://twitter.com/CCBalert/status/2074766432739496260)), CVE-2026-39999 ([zoomeye_team](https://twitter.com/zoomeye_team/status/2075131427679854592)).
- **기타**: 이번 수집 데이터 내 도메인 및 해시값은 특정되지 않음.

### 6. 즉각 대응 권고
- **Microsoft Defender 업데이트**: CVE-2026-50656 대응을 위해 Malware Protection Engine을 최신 버전(1.1.26060.3008 이상)으로 즉시 업데이트할 것.

📈 세션 누적: 14,883 / 1,000,000 (1.5%) | 세션 잔여 추정 985,117개

news_articles.title: 로앤컴퍼니, 글로벌 AI 학회서 법률 LLM 추론 연구 논문 발표
news_articles.summary: 리걸테크 기업 로앤컴퍼니는 법률 영역에서의 AI 모델 추론 연구 성과를 담은 논문이 국제머신러닝학회인 ICML 2026의 AI4Law 워크숍에 채택됐다고 9일 밝혔다.ICML은 인공지능 및 머신러닝 분야의 주요 학술대회다. 이번에 논문이 발표되는 AI4Law 워크숍은 법률 추론과 법률 분야 AI 평가 등을 다루는 행사로 오는 10일 서울 코엑스에서 개최된다.로앤컴퍼니는 'AI Achieves a Perfect LSAT Score'라는 논문을 통해 LLM 모델 추론 과정의 중요성을 입증했다. 연구진은 8개 AI 모델을 대상으로 미국

news_articles.title: 2026년 2분기 리눅스 SSH 서버 대상 악성코드 통계 보고서
news_articles.summary: 내용 2026년 2분기 AhnLab SEcurity intelligence Center(ASEC)은 허니팟을 통해 부적절하게 관리되는 리눅스 SSH 서버를 대상으로 한 공격 로그를 수집하고 분석했다. 분석 범위는 실제 악성코드 설치 명령까지 수행된 공격지와 그 공격에 사용된 악성코드 통계이다. 목적 및 범위 이 보고서는 리눅스 SSH 서버를 겨냥한 무차별 대입 공격(Brute Forcing)과 사전 공격(Dictionary Attack)의 현황, 공격에 사용된 악성코드 분류, 그리고 […]

news_articles.title: 2026년 2분기 윈도우 웹 서버 대상 악성코드 통계 보고서
news_articles.summary: 내용 2026년 2분기 AhnLab SEcurity intelligence Center(ASEC)은 부적절하게 관리되는 Windows 웹 서버에 대한 공격 현황과 악성코드 분류를 정리했다. 대상은 Windows 환경의 Internet Information Services(IIS) 웹 서버와 Apache Tomcat 웹 서버이다. 목적 및 범위 이 보고서는 2026년 2분기에 확인된 Windows 웹 서버 대상 공격의 피해 현황, 공격 횟수, 사용된 악성코드 분류, 웹쉘 통계, 실제 공격 사례를 […]

news_articles.title: 2026년 2분기 윈도우 데이터베이스 서버 대상 악성코드 통계 보고서
news_articles.summary: 내용 AhnLab SEcurity intelligence Center(ASEC)은 2026년 2분기 윈도우에 설치된 MS-SQL 서버와 MySQL 서버를 대상으로 한 공격 로그를 분석했다. 이 보고서는 피해 현황과 공격 현황, 그리고 공격에 사용된 악성코드와 도구의 분류를 정리한다. 목적 및 범위 대상은 MS-SQL 서버와 MySQL 서버이다. 공격은 주로 보안 패치가 되지 않은 환경, 부적절한 설정, 부적절한 계정 관리 환경을 노린다. 보고서는 공격에 […]
